issue_title: Improper URL formation on #iefix font file call
issue_body:
On [this line](https://github.com/FortAwesome/Font-Awesome/blob/c6e79ae1a1e7bef4e7290e5026b704d24f4fa590/css/font-awesome.css#L10):

`url('../fonts/fontawesome-webfont.svg?v=4.6.3#fontawesomeregular)` is properly formatted according to [RFC 3986.4.2](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc3986#section-4.2), with the fragment coming after the query string.

`url('../fonts/fontawesome-webfont.eot?#iefix&v=4.6.3')` is improperly formatted, with the query string coming *inside* the fragment, where a lot of servers won't properly parse it.

issue_title: Add tags to replicas to support many replicas
issue_body:
Replicas should have the following attributes available.  This would be added as tags within the replica description, both in the API and in the SIS.  If the tags are absent, default behavior remains the same; in the config, the tags below are set to True or False:

**nofailover**: this replica should not be a candidate for automatic failover.  Used for ephemeral replicas, and multi-replica setups where there are a few designated failover targets.   If the tag is missing, the replica is assumed to be a failover target.

**noloadbalance**: this replica should not be part of any load-balancing, if HAProxy autoconfig is being used.  

**clonefrom**:  take basebackups from this replica *instead of* from the master.  If multiple CloneFrom replicals are available, one is randomly chosen for new basebackups.  If cloning using WALE or other archival backup, this tag is ignored.

**replicatefrom**: not true or false, but the IP address/hostname of another replica.  Used to support cascading replication.

issue_title: Incorrect set http response data when returning flask.Response objects
issue_body:
### Description

Incorrect set http response data when returning flask.Response objects

### Expected bahaviour

From the [documentation](https://connexion.readthedocs.io/en/latest/response.html#response-serialization):

> "If the endpoint returns a Response object this response will be used as is."

### Actual behaviour

The response is parsed in connexion.decorators.decorator.BaseDecorator.get_full_response() and the [response object itself is mistakenly set as the response's data instead of response.data](https://github.com/zalando/connexion/blob/master/connexion/decorators/decorator.py#L27):

This later throws a JSON serialize error when trying to [JSON serialize the data variable](https://github.com/zalando/connexion/blob/master/connexion/decorators/response.py#L47) (mistaken Response object)

### Steps to reproduce



### Additional info:

Output of the commands:

- `python --version`
Python 2.7.12
- `pip show connexion | grep "^Version\:"`
Version: 1.0.129

issue_title: JSON encoding of Decimal fails
issue_body:
### Description

Decimal values could be returned from DB queries.
This issue could be fixed by patching ``app.json_encoder``, but I thought it could be useful to be part of the base connexion encoder.

```python
TypeError: Decimal('0') is not JSON serializable
```

### Expected behaviour

API responds with valid JSON including float values (e.g. ``0.1`` or ``1.1e-05`` etc ...)

### Actual behaviour

JSON serialization fail

### Steps to reproduce

```python
import json
from decimal import Decimal
from connexion.decorators.produces import JSONEncoder

json.dumps(Decimal(0.1), cls=JSONEncoder)
```

### Additional info:
http://www.json.org/
http://stackoverflow.com/questions/19554972/json-standard-floating-point-numbers

Output of the commands:

- `python --version`
``Python 3.5.2``

- `pip show connexion | grep "^Version\:"`
``Version: 1.1.4``

issue_title: Feature request: hook for running script each time the Guake window become visible
issue_body:
Guake is running well here on Ubuntu 16.04 except for little thing: I use an international keyboard layout in the desktop environment but for the terminal I want to use the US keyboard. I have made the layouts available and can switch between them by a key combination.
However, I have to do this anew each time Guake becomes visible. For some reason the layout is always reset to the standard one. That becomes tiresome quite soon. So I was wondering if there was a way to issue a script for each tab every time that Guake becomes visible.
I have tried the startup script option but of course that script is only run once and does not help much in my case. Also, I toyed around with the idea of running 'guake -s $TAB -e' for each tab but that executes the command directly in the shell which is quite intrusive. Toggling the visibility ten times would clutter each tab with ten identical calls to a script.
Maybe even simpler: would it be possible to select the keyboard layout in the settings?
Cheers!

issue_body:
_Issue by **[benjello](https://github.com/benjello)** from Saturday Mar 01, 2014 at 00:09 GMT_
_Originally opened as https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-core/issues/211_

---

_Issue by **[AlexisEidelman](https://github.com/AlexisEidelman)** from Thursday May 30, 2013 at 15:32 GMT_
_Originally opened as https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca/issues/212_

---

I try to clean the get value function. 
Doing this I figured out that there is might be a problem with actual code and alloc in irpp.

issue_title: Add back fov=60 to camera
Timeout returns empty result instead of error
issue_body:
<!--- Verify first that your issue/request has not already been reported -->

##### Issue type
<!--- Pick one below and delete the rest: -->
 - Bug Report

##### OverPy version
<!--- Paste verbatim output from “python -c 'import overpy; print(overpy.__version__)'” or the hash of the git commit between quotes below. -->
```
0.4
```

##### OS
<!---
Add information about the os you are using OverPy on.
Pick one below and delete the rest:
-->
 - OSX 10.12.3

#### Python version
<!-- Pick at least one below and delete the rest -->
 - Python 3.5

##### Summary
<!--- Explain the problem briefly. -->
When setting a short timeout (1-30s), such that the timeout is reached, an empty result is returned instead of a timeout error. Looking into the query() function code determines that there is a <remark> describing the timeout in the xml result, and that the error code '200' is raised, but the parse_xml() function is being used to resolve it to an empty result. A similar issue is discussed in the Overpass API [#94](https://github.com/drolbr/Overpass-API/issues/94).
##### Steps to reproduce
<!---
Try to describe the steps how to reproduce the issue.
-->
Run a short timeout query like
```
[out:xml][timeout:10];
area["name"="France"]->.searchArea;
(
node["amenity"="embassy"](area.searchArea);
way["amenity"="embassy"](area.searchArea);
relation["amenity"="embassy"](area.searchArea);
);
out center;
```
via
`result = overpy.Overpass().query(query))`
<!--- You can also add links to gist.github.com. -->

##### Expected results
<!--- What did you expect to happen? -->
raise overpy.exception.OverpassGatewayTimeout or some relevant error
##### Actual results
<!--- What actually happened? -->
returns a result for which
```
print(result.get_nodes())
print(result.get_ways())
print(result.get_relations())
```
returns an empty list [] for each.

XML result looked like:
```
b'<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>\n<osm version="0.6" generator="Overpass API">\n<note>The data included in this document is from www.openstreetmap.org. The data is made available under ODbL.</note>\n<meta osm_base="2017-03-17T15:40:02Z" areas="2017-03-17T06:44:02Z"/>\n\n<remark> runtime error: Query timed out in "query" at line 1 after 2 seconds. </remark>\n\n</osm>\n'

f.code: 200
```

issue_title: Using bsonjs for GridFS
issue_body:
Hi, 

First of all, it looks like an extremely interesting library that could really improve some of the performance issues in pymongo with larger documents! So this might be maybe more of a question maybe than an issue, but this might help other people too. 

So normally if I put something into gridfs, i use bson.json_util.dumps to do a conversion:  
dict -> json string -> bson binary. 
From what I read on pymongo, bsonjs is a stand-in for it (with less options but much better performance). However as far as i can see, bsonjs doesn't do exactly the same as json_util but only the  
json string -> bson binary
So I need to construct the json string first myself, for which i can use bson.json_util or another library like ujson:

    %timeit a = bson.json_util.dumps(nested_dict)
    1 loop, best of 3: 255 ms per loop

    %timeit a = ujson.dumps(nested_dict)
    10 loops, best of 3: 24.4 ms per loop

So this makes it about 10x faster, which is great. But I cannot always use ujson (because of ObjectIds) and bsonjs itself falls over encodings in strings which i cannot control with ujson. 

Question 1: Unless I have a quick way to generate the json string, there is no real advantage to it? So i need to work with these constraints right?

Question 2: Is there any way to also write raw documents to GridFS like you documented for the document_class? Or does that happen automatically if the used db is in Raw BSON mode?

Question 3: How does it work for (non-GridFS, normal collections) updates? do i need to encode the {'_id': ObjectId('123456789')}, {'$set': {'foo': 'bar'}} as two raw bson documents?

Thanks!

issue_title: Allow lambda settings outside of config.json
issue_body:
From my understanding, the `lambda_timeout` and `lambda_memory_size` values in the config file will always apply to any lambdas generated by chalice. Obviously this includes the main lambda for the application, but also applies to lambdas created using scheduled events.

It would be nice to be able to overwrite lambda settings defined in the config file for such events. A use case could be a daily scheduled event that is expected to run all functions in your app and requires a longer timeout and/or more memory than an individual route in your app.

By the way, chalice is a pleasure to use. Thanks!

https://github.com/aws/chalice/blob/1cdebd90d66435c26958687a1d6a3670eeb06293/chalice/deploy/packager.py#L84-L88

issue_title: Enable Travis CI test for pip installation on OSX
issue_body:
#596 enabled Travis CI test for pip installation on Linux, but on OSX it doesn't work because `libdynet.dylib` is not found. This can be resolved by adding its location to `DYLD_LIBRARY_PATH`. It's not `$TRAVIS_BUILD_DIR/build/dynet` because the pip installation creates a build directory inside `build` called something like `py3.4-64bit`, but instead of trying to figure that out, it should be possible to use the virtualenv, something like `$(dirname $(which python))/../lib` because it installs it into that location.

I've been trying forever to make the Python bindings work on OSX without requiring `DYLD_LIBRARY_PATH`, but I can't figure out how to add the lib properly to `RUNTIME_LIBRARY_DIRS`. Perhaps someone with more Mac experience can help.

issue_title: Document current glyph name syntax
issue_body: According to [§2.f.i of the OpenType Feature File syntax](http://www.adobe.com/devnet/opentype/afdko/topic_feature_file_syntax.html#2.f.i), glyph names must not contain hyphens. However, @readroberts wrote in https://github.com/fonttools/fonttools/issues/559#issuecomment-224007849 that makeotf has been changed to accept glyph names with `- + * : ~ ^ !`. This was done so that makeotf could support workflows where features get compiled on fonts that have “development” glyph names. Could the spec be amended to reflect what’s currently accepted? The spec might still want to contain a warning that when a font uses these characters, it will contain invalid PostScript and Type 1 names.

issue_title: [Suggestion] Base64 script
issue_body:
[I have asked on the German Python forum](https://www.python-forum.de/viewtopic.php?f=1&t=38540&p=295424#p295424) for help with regard to a [Python base64 script](https://github.com/jcul/b64), that I have found on GitHub a few days ago.

Sirius3, a member of that forum, has filled my request and agrees with my proposal, to integrate that script into the AFDKO.

Because the AFDKO (with Fonttools included) contains almost all for preparation of fonts for the web, I thought, it would be cool, if the AFDKO would contain a base64 script. Then you have all for preparing fonts for the web except from eotfast.

The license is the same as the original license of b64.py: [GNU General Public License, version 2](http://www.gnu.org/licenses/old-licenses/gpl-2.0.en.html).

Here is the script:

```
from __future__ import print_function
import argparse
import base64
 
ENCODERS = [
    (base64.standard_b64encode, base64.standard_b64decode),
    (base64.urlsafe_b64encode, base64.urlsafe_b64decode),
]
 
def parse_args(args=None):
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description='Encode and Decode base64.')
   
    group = parser.add_mutually_exclusive_group(required=True)
    group.add_argument('-e', '--encode', action='store_false', dest='decode')
    group.add_argument('-d', '--decode', action='store_true')
   
    parser.add_argument('--urlsafe', action='store_true', help='Encode urlsafe')
    parser.add_argument('--line-length', help='Defaults to unlimited', default=0, type=int)
 
    parser.add_argument('-i', '--infile', help='Defaults to stdin')
    parser.add_argument('-o', '--outfile', help='Defaults to stdout')
    parser.add_argument('args', nargs='*')
   
    return parser.parse_args(args)
 
def insert_newlines(string, every=64):
    return '\n'.join(string[i:i+every] for i in xrange(0, len(string), every))
 
def get_input(opts):
    if opts.infile is None:
        instr = ' '.join(opts.args).encode()
    else:
        with open(opts.infile, 'r' if opts.decode else 'rb') as ifh:
            instr = ifh.read()
    return instr
   
def write_output(opts, outstr):
    if opts.outfile is None:
        print(outstr)
    else:
        with open(opts.outfile, 'wb' if opts.decode else 'w') as ofh:
            ofh.write(outstr)
 
def main():
    opts = parse_args()
    instr = get_input(opts)
    outstr = ENCODERS[opts.urlsafe][opts.decode](instr)
    if opts.line_length > 0 and not opts.decode:
        outstr = insert_newlines(outstr, opts.line_length)
    write_output(opts, outstr)
 
if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()
```

The comment in `gcloud.streaming.transfer.Download.configure_request` (inherited from apitools) says:

```python
        # We send range requests because by default httplib2 stores entire
        # reponses in memory.  Consider overriding httplib2's download method
        # (as gsutil does) so that this is not necessary.
```

issue_title: Query with projection on datetime entity doesn't deserialize to datetime
issue_body:
If you do a query and the projection has a datetime field, for instance:

```python
entity = client.entity(client.key('example'))
entity['created'] = datetime.datetime.utcnow()

query = client.query(kind='example')
query.projection = ['created']
results = list(query.fetch())
```

The `results[0]['created']` will be a timestamp instead of a datetime object.

issue_title: Noisy logging.error: "Bad state for purpose"
issue_body:
When debugging aborted pipelines, we tend to look to error logs with `path:pipeline` on the console.

In this search, you will find errors like the following:
`Pipeline ID "23377674aae54a8b883cb75636a8fcf6" in bad state for purpose "abort": "aborted"`

This specific message seems to me to be a bit verbose, as I'd expect this to be an okay situation.

----

There are perhaps 2 points of discussion worth having here:

1. The obvious, why is an aborted pipeline being triggered for abortion?
2. The more helpful, can we disable this particular log from emitting?

----

If the community finds it appropriate, I've tackled item 2 in #51 and its ready to be merged. 
If not, we can keep the change to our fork at github.com/Loudr/appengine-pipelines . :beers:

issue_title: Snippet in documentation is not correct.
issue_body:
Code snippet on [PubSub index page](https://google-cloud-python.readthedocs.io/en/latest/pubsub/index.html#subscribing):

```python
def callback(message):
    print(message.data)
    message.ack()
future = subscriber.subscribe(subscription_name, callback)
```
But, subscribe returns a `Policy` instance not `future`. 

```python
def callback(message):
    print(message.data)
    message.ack()
policy = subscriber.subscribe(subscription_name, callback)
future = policy.future
```

Is more appropriate

issue_title: Pubsub.  Unexpected behaviour. Pubsub client does not pull messages it has previously processed without acking.
issue_body:
macOS High Sierra 10.13.3. 
Python 3.6.2. 
google-cloud-core 0.28.1.

`import logging
import re
import sys
import time

from google.cloud import pubsub_v1

logger = logging.getLogger(__file__)

PROJECT_ID = 'my_id'
SUBSCRIPTION_NAME = 'my_subscription'

def run():
    client = pubsub_v1.SubscriberClient()
    sub_name = 'projects/{}/subscriptions/{}'.format(PROJECT_ID, SUBSCRIPTION_NAME)
    subscription = client.subscribe(
        subscription=sub_name,
        callback=callback
    )
    while True:
        time.sleep(5)

def callback(message):
    success = process_message(message)
    if success:
        message.ack()
    # else:
    #     message.nack()

def process_message(message):
    print(message)
    return False

if __name__ == '__main__':
    run()
`

When I run this code, my client pulls messages on the queue once. It then does not ack the message, and the message returns to the queue after 10 seconds as expected. After they return to the queue, the client does however not pull the message again. If I explicitly nack the message, it does pull the message from the queue again.

issue_title: 'objects.rewrite' incompatible with 'user_project'
issue_body:
Our library functions which use the [`objects.rewrite` endpoint](https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/json_api/v1/objects/rewrite) cannot currently be used with `user_project` set on the object's bucket:  the back-end returns errors for such requests.

@frankyn reports that the issue is known, and that a fix is pending review / release by the back-end team.

issue_title: Cognito StringAttributeConstraints should be MinLength/MaxLength not MinValue/MaxValue
issue_body:
There is bug with the definition of `cognito.StringAttributeConstraints`

with the following code:
```
class StringAttributeConstraints(AWSProperty):
    props = {
        'MaxValue': (basestring, False),
        'MinValue': (basestring, False),
    }
```

According to the AWS document
http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSCloudFormation/latest/UserGuide/aws-properties-cognito-userpool-stringattributeconstraints.html

The properties should be:
- MaxLength
- MinLength

not 
- MaxValue
- MinValue

If there are non-string values ```self._distributions``` [this](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pex/blob/master/pex/pex_builder.py#L214) line does not work:
```
direvius@debuild-trusty:~/robe/yandex-tank$ pex -f wheelhouse/ yandextank -c yandex.tank -o yandex-tank.pex
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/local/bin/pex", line 11, in <module>
    sys.exit(main())
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/pex/bin/pex.py", line 509, in main
    pex_builder = build_pex(reqs, options, resolver_options_builder)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/pex/bin/pex.py", line 486, in build_pex
    pex_builder.set_script(options.script)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/pex/pex_builder.py", line 214, in set_script
    script, ', '.join(self._distributions)))
TypeError: sequence item 0: expected string, DistInfoDistribution found
```
The solution is to convert them into strings before joining:
```
', '.join(str(d) for d in self._distributions)
```

issue_title: Build cache behavior interacts badly with unclaimed classes behavior of jvm compile
issue_body:
Current buildcache / compile behavior allows for stale class files from incremental compiles to be added to the buildcache for a newer version of a target.

Setup
target A has compile output, C, from a previous compile run with fingerprint F
target A is modified such that it matches an earlier fingerprint F1 that is cached as C1

compile target A
- hits the buildcache, sees F1
- extracts C1 into the work dir
- skips zinc compile, which causes the compile outputs C to be left in place


result
- Both C and C1 are in the work dir even though C isn't a product of the current sources
- Another change + compile will cause C to be considered 'unclaimed' classes and stuffed into the classes_by_target product and from there into the buildcache, which means the C artifacts will survive a clean-all. :(

workaround:
- turn off local build cache writing, and ensure CI jobs run clean-alls prior to interacting with new snapshots of the workspace

solutions:
- un-extract cached files if the previous fingerprint was cached when extracting a new fingerprint's cache
- change how unclaimed classes are cached, maybe ensure annotation processors changes are checked somehow, and those classes are assigned to them.
- allow tasks to specify behavior prior to extracting cached artifacts--ie isolated compile could clean out the class / analysis directories for a compilation unit prior to having the cache extracted there. 


I think the most common case is that C is actually also a cached version of A, so my initial work is toward dealing with that since that covers the case that we got a report for.

issue_title: investigate high memory utilization with v2 engine, daemon
issue_body:
essentially, with a `./pants list ::` in our monorepo the rss of the daemon process stays on the order of 4-7GB and can balloon +/-.5..3GB on each subsequent run (seen as high as 12GB). this seems problematic on its own - but additionally, this sheer memory footprint size seems to cause a noticeable pause when `DaemonPantsRunner` forks under these conditions. afaict, approximately 20% of the time of a `./pants list ::` is spent just forking.

this [screen cap](https://asciinema.org/a/4qzul93k5svt07bf6i67dvd2u) demonstrates the crux of the problem, as captured by `ps`. the capture starts with a primed daemon (a complete `./pants list ::`) and then illustrates the memory use behavior of multiple subsequent `./pants list ::` executed via the daemon. the pause noted between emission of the "we're forking" log message and actual output in the thin client is correlated to a ballooning memory footprint of the forked process as seen in the screencap.

issue_title: 1.0.0(-rc1) changelog not available on the docsite
issue_body:
The notes for 1.0.0 are not included on the docsite, because they never existed in master. We should cherry-pick the notes to master as a one time fix.

But to prevent this issue in the future, we'll need to change the release process documentation to always require separate reviews for the notes and the version bump.

Proposal:
1. One review to merge notes to `master` (regardless of release branch)
2. A separate review to bump the version in the relevant branch (perhaps with a special case to allow for using just one review in the case of a snapshot bump)

issue_title: save_all=True produces static GIF
issue_body:
I'm not sure how to actually create an animated GIF with PIL, so I've resorted to using Image.save like so:

```
from PIL import Image, ImageSequence

f1= Image.new("RGB", (100, 100), "white")
f2= Image.new("RGB", (100, 100), "black")

p= 'test.gif'
f1.save('test.gif', 'GIF', save_all=True, append_images=[f2])

i= Image.open(p)
print('number of saved frames:', len(list(ImageSequence.Iterator(i))))
```

I've confirmed that this does actually call the _save_all function in from GifImagePlugin, and the append_images parameter is also passed there correctly. Even so, the saved GIF only has 1 frame. The code snippet also counts only 1 frame.

MWE: Travel times from University of Denver to Denver International Airport. Should be ~40 minutes, but should vary substantially with traffic. 

```
result = gmaps.directions('39.6766215,-104.9640905', 
                 '39.8561004,-104.6759316', 
                 mode='driving',
                 departure_time=datetime.datetime(2018, 11, 6, 12, 00))

result = gmaps.directions('39.6766215,-104.9640905', 
                 '39.8561004,-104.6759316', 
                 mode='driving',
                 departure_time=datetime.datetime(2018, 11, 6, 8, 30))

result = gmaps.directions('39.6766215,-104.9640905', 
                 '39.8561004,-104.6759316', 
                 mode='driving',
                 departure_time=datetime.datetime(2018, 11, 6, 17, 30))
print('time in minutes rushhour2: {}'.format(result[0]['legs'][0]['duration']['value']/60))

issue_title: Add GCP support
issue_body:
With the addition of GCP's Cloud KMS API it should be possible to natively support Confidant in GCP. There's a few major differences between GCP's KMS and AWS's KMS, though:

1. Though GCP's KMS support AAD through encrypt/decrypt, GCP's IAM does not support restricting access based on the AAD.
2. GCP's KMS has no quota on the number of keys, just on the number of operations. Also, each key can have IAM policy attached to it.
3. GCP's KMS has no random function.
4. GCP's KMS does not support functions like create data key. To do envelope encryption it's necessary to generate random data from the VM's urandom, and then encrypt that random data. We can't seed urandom using KMS. It's possible the entropy will be lower in GCP.
5. GCP's KMS does not have grants, but using a key per service would be analogous to how we use grants.

The differences between AWS's KMS and GCP's KMS mostly affect how we do service to service authentication. For service to service auth, we'd need to rely on a crypto key per service, rather than relying on encryption context for data on a single key. At-rest encryption should be possible with few modifications.

For data storage we'd need to support something other than DynamoDB. The likely replacement is [datastore](https://cloud.google.com/datastore/).

issue_title: Make dark.reads.Reads.filter return a new Reads instance
issue_body:
It would be slightly more convenient, I think, if `dark.reads.Reads.filter` returned a new `Reads` instance instead of a generator that yields `Read` instances. Then you could do this:
```python
reads = FastaReads(filename).filter(...)
```
and you'd have a `Reads` instance. Right now (once #349 is merged) you'd need to
```python
reads = Reads(FastaReads(filename).filter(...))
```
I think that, given the user is already using a `Reads` instance, we know that they want to work with that class, so returning one from `filter` makes sense.

issue_title: Proposal: A new user-focused API
issue_body:
### Problem

The current API is a great wrapper around the REST API, but many common tasks that users want to do don't necessarily map directly to REST API calls. For example, simply running a container is [a rather convoluted process](https://gist.github.com/bfirsh/d3435b3f26033dd533cc4732639ae2ef#file-getting-started-python-md).
### Proposal

I am proposing we add a higher-level API focused on what users want to do. It should be the default, recommended way of using docker-py.

It could look something like this:

``` python
>>> import docker
>>> client = docker.from_env()

>>> client.containers.run("ubuntu", "echo hello world")
'hello world\n'

>>> client.containers.run("ubuntu", "tasks/reticulate-splines", detach=True)
<Container '45e6d2de7c54'>

>>> client.containers.list()
[<Container '45e6d2de7c54'>, <Container 'db18e4f20eaa'>, ...]

>>> container = client.containers.get('45e6d2de7c54')

>>> for line in container.logs(stream=True):
...   print line
Reticulating spline 2...
Reticulating spline 3...
```

[See this sketch of a README for a more examples of how it could work.](https://github.com/bfirsh/docker-py/blob/user-api/README.md) It intentionally starts with a common thing that users want to do which is complex with the current API: running containers.
### Backwards compatibility

This is a good excuse for 2.0. ;)

The new API can be built on top of the current lower-level API. The current API can be made available to users in case they want that extra flexibility and to give them a seamless migration path (`client.api.create_container(...)` or something like that).
### See also
- This is building on top of @shin-'s work in #788.
- Related issues: #1049 #417 
- [dockerrun](https://github.com/bfirsh/dockerrun), an example of how a `run()` method could work.

issue_title: __str__ bug in TabularCPD
DBN Bug: multiple CPDs for the same varaiable
issue_body:
I found this problem while working on #452 
```python
from pgmpy.models import DynamicBayesianNetwork as dbn
from pgmpy.factors import TabularCPD
import numpy as np
model = dbn()
model.add_edges_from([(('a',0),('b',1)),(('c',0),('b',1))])
values = np.split(np.random.rand(4),2)
a_cpd = TabularCPD(('b',1), 2, values, [('a',0)], 2)
c_cpd = TabularCPD(('b',1), 2, values, [('c',0)], 2)
model.add_cpds(a_cpd, c_cpd)
model.initialize_initial_state()
```
Here the 2 CPDs are for the same variable , when `self.check_model()` is done in [here](https://github.com/pgmpy/pgmpy/blob/dev/pgmpy/models/DynamicBayesianNetwork.py#L517)
This error will be raised

```python
if set(evidence) != set(parents if parents else []):
                    raise ValueError("CPD associated with {node} doesn't have "
                                     "proper parents associated with it.".format(node=node))` 
```
Because the number of parents will be more than the number of evidences.

If you confirmed that this is a problem, i think it can be solved simply by applying CPD product for all CPDs of the same variable.

issue_title: Independency missing in get_independencies()
issue_body:
For an example Bayesian network specified on page 4 of the book, Probabilistic Graphical Models by Daphne Koller which is as follows
![flu_bn](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/8504351/19075598/4012f460-8a63-11e6-9057-256d7ae25031.jpg)
Now as per the book, some of the conditional independencies are as follows.
```
 (F _|_ H | S)
 (C _|_ S | F, H)
 (M _|_ H, C | F)
 (M _|_ C | F)
```
Now if we use the ```get_independencies``` method of Bayesian network, we get the following result
```
>>> from pgmpy.models import BayesianModel as bm
>>> G = bm()
>>> G.add_edges_from([('S','F'), ('S','H'), ('F','C'), ('H','C'),('F','M')])
>>> G.edges()
[('S', 'F'), ('S', 'H'), ('H', 'C'), ('F', 'C'), ('F', 'M')]
>>> G.get_independencies()
(S _|_ F, C, M | H)
(S _|_ H, C | F)
(S _|_ F, H, C | M)
(S _|_ F, H, M | C)
(H _|_ C | S)
(H _|_ S, C | F)
(H _|_ F, C, S | M)
(H _|_ M, F, S | C)
(F _|_ C, M | S)
(F _|_ S, C, M | H)
(F _|_ S, H, C | M)
(F _|_ S, H, M | C)
(M _|_ F, C | S)
(M _|_ F, C, S | H)
(M _|_ F, S, H | C)
(C _|_ F, H, M | S)
(C _|_ M, F, S | H)
(C _|_ S, H | F)
(C _|_ F, H, S | M)
```
The conditional independency ``` (F _|_H | S)``` is missing.

issue_title: Add default parameter metadata to NI-SWITCH
issue_body:
Go through the API with a domain expert, and decide for each function which parameters should have default values in the Python API and what those values should be.

This needs to be done carefully, because in Python, all parameters with default values must go at the end of the parameter list. So changing this later would likely be a source breaker for client code.

This will most likely be a subset of Recommended/Optional input terminals on corresponding LabVIEW VIs.

issue_title: Event publishing inside long procedure gets blocked
Add support for >300x300 screen resolution
issue_body:
It seems this is not a Unity limitation based on playing around with Chalet which also uses Unity but can do smaller than 300x300.
Benefits of adding lower resolution: Faster, supports network architectures trained on 224x224 imagenet size or 84x84 Atari size
Drawbacks: You don't get all the pretty pixels we worked so hard to make.

I remember we had some weird problems before with calling this function causing renderers to not show up and getting big red patches, but I think that may have been fixed by a Unity update.
https://docs.unity3d.com/ScriptReference/Screen.SetResolution.html

issue_title: It is ok to have all zero grads sometimes
issue_body:
Consider a case, where you have some RNN that embeds a sentence that consists of only one word.
In the simplest case, it has two sets of parameters: input2hidden, and hidded2hidden. In our case we have only one timestep, so no grads are computed for hidden2hidden parameters, and thus the test below will fail, despite the behavior is intended. https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/blob/4cda858012261c5fa5f668236abaeb4333d587ec/allennlp/common/testing/model_test_case.py#L119-L121

issue_title: prefetch_related() that uses a Prefetch with a queryset is not joining correctly
issue_body:
Here is a failing test that can be added to `versions_tests.tests.test_models.PrefetchingHistoricTests`:
```
    def test_foreign_key_prefetch_with_historic_version(self):
        self.modify_objects()
        historic_city = City.objects.as_of(self.time1).get(identity=self.c1.identity)

        # Test with a simple prefetch
        team = Team.objects.as_of(self.time1).filter(identity=self.t1.identity).prefetch_related('city')[0]
        self.assertIsNotNone(team.city)
        self.assertEquals(team.city.id, historic_city.id)

        # Test with a Prefetch object without a queryset.
        team = Team.objects.as_of(self.time1).filter(
            identity=self.t1.identity
        ).prefetch_related(Prefetch(
            'city',
        ))[0]
        self.assertIsNotNone(team.city)
        self.assertEquals(team.city.id, historic_city.id)

        # Test with a Prefetch object with a queryset.
        team = Team.objects.as_of(self.time1).filter(
            identity=self.t1.identity
        ).prefetch_related(Prefetch(
            'city',
            queryset=City.objects.as_of(self.time1)
        ))[0]
        # This is currently failing:
        self.assertIsNotNone(team.city)
        self.assertEquals(team.city.id, historic_city.id)
```

The second-to-last assertion fails.

Inspecting the generated query, I can see that it tries to select the related objects using ids.  It should however select using identities and an as_of clause.

I suspect that the `VersionedReverseSingleRelatedObjectDescriptor` needs to have a custom `get_prefetch_queryset()` implementation.

It probably makes sense to merge the Django-1.9-compatibility code before fixing this.

issue_title: Go-based /bin/boot scripts output warnings for "Key already exists"
issue_body:
In the new Go-based /bin/boot binaries, when we make sure etcd directories exist, we don't swallow the error if they do exist. Users see output like this:

```
[WARNING] - 105: Key already exists (/deis/controller) [15]
[WARNING] - 105: Key already exists (/deis/services) [15]
[WARNING] - 105: Key already exists (/deis/domains) [15]
[WARNING] - 105: Key already exists (/deis/builder) [15]
```

We'd really like to swallow these errors, like we do in the bash-based /bin/boot scripts:

```
function etcd_safe_mkdir {
	set +e
	etcdctl --no-sync -C $ETCD mkdir $1 >/dev/null 2>&1
	if [[ $? -ne 0 && $? -ne 4 ]]; then
		echo "etcd_safe_mkdir: an etcd error occurred. aborting..."
		exit 1
	fi
	set -e
}
```

Note here we interpret the exit code of `etcdctl`. It should be fairly easy to do this with the vendored code, as we can check the type of error directly.

issue_title: Remove scheduler timeouts / Taking too long to pull a run container fails with 'failed to create container'
issue_body:
Debugging Deis on Azure led us to initially suspect an Azure-specific issue, but we have seen this on other platforms. Any time a `deis run` takes longer than 60 seconds to complete the `docker pull`, the client errs with `failed to create container`.

This leads to a deeper issue with our timeouts -- the router uses a [20 minute timeout](https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/f03078180bd830cec7a23bd67acfc19e5f966f42/router/image/templates/nginx.conf#L152-L153). In the fleet scheduling logic, we have various loops which perform actions:

* [_wait_for_container](https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/f03078180bd830cec7a23bd67acfc19e5f966f42/controller/scheduler/fleet.py#L163)
* [_wait_for_destroy](https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/f03078180bd830cec7a23bd67acfc19e5f966f42/controller/scheduler/fleet.py#L180)
* [run](https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/f03078180bd830cec7a23bd67acfc19e5f966f42/controller/scheduler/fleet.py#L216)
* [wait for launch](https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/f03078180bd830cec7a23bd67acfc19e5f966f42/controller/scheduler/fleet.py#L260)
* [wait for start](https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/f03078180bd830cec7a23bd67acfc19e5f966f42/controller/scheduler/fleet.py#L269)
* [wait for completion](https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/f03078180bd830cec7a23bd67acfc19e5f966f42/controller/scheduler/fleet.py#L282)

We really shouldn't be "budgeting" the 20 minutes across these timeouts, as we have no idea how long any one will take at any one time. For example, on Azure, a `docker pull` for my run container took 5 minutes, but our pollwait loop only waits for 60s. Letting each of these loops run indefinitely and instead letting the worker/router handle the total request timeout is probably preferred here.

issue_title: `task event-list` filters aren't mutually exclusive, but cancel out -- improve doc or change behavior
issue_body:
In its latest version, `task event-list` takes `--filter-errors` and `--filter-non-errors`.
These don't raise an error if both given, and the behavior when that happens (do no filtering) is potentially confusing.

Either we should doc that giving both options annuls them, or we should make them mutually exclusive.

issue_title: Have the solver work on a set of packages states, for a series of independent conda actions
issue_body:
Assuming ther was an api (just for easier explanation)

```python
from conda import api
some_package_state = api.install(packages=[some-package], dry=True, env='root', state_output=True)
packages that would live in the env after the command
other_pacakge_state = api.update(packages=[some-other-package], state=somestate)
final_package_state = api.remove(packages=[some-other-other-package], state=otherstate)
```

The initial command is applied on an env (root) but using the dry run flag and the state_output, so that the output is a state (which is the name I am giving to a set of packages that includes before and after plus differences), like

```python
state = {
    'before': set(<package>),
    'after': set(<packages>),
    'removed': set(<packages>),
    'upgraded': set(<packages>),
    'downgraded': (<packages>),
}
```

This is useful for Navigator, as we allow users to perform several action on different packages, (install, update, downgrade, remove) so having a final state of changes for the user to know before actually applying the commands is not possible.

This recipe fails:

``` yaml

issue_title: Unsatisfiability hint code can take too long
conda raises bogus NotWritableError exception for a bogus attempt to chown to root
issue_body:
I am attempting to upgrade an existing installation of conda by running `conda update conda anaconda-client` (though the specific command does not appear to be important, anything that results in changes to `~/.conda`, i.e. practically any command, has the same problem). This results in the following error: ``` NotWritableError: The current user does not have write permissions to a required path. path: /var/lib/buildbot/.conda/environments.txt uid: 104 gid: 111 If you feel that permissions on this path are set incorrectly, you can manually change them by executing $ sudo chown 104:111 /var/lib/buildbot/.conda/environments.txt In general, it's not advisable to use 'sudo conda'. ``` Running the same command with `strace -eopen,chown -f` prepended shows the following relevant syscalls: ``` [pid 4612] open("/var/lib/buildbot/.conda/environments.txt", O_WRONLY|O_CREAT|O_APPEND, 0666) = 8 [pid 4612] chown("/var/lib/buildbot/.conda/environments.txt", 0, 0) = -1 EPERM (Operation not permitted) ``` Two things are wrong here. 1. The error message is completely wrong. The file *is* writable by the current user, and the permissions are set correctly. 2. Conda is trying to change the owner of a file when it is not root. The reason Conda is trying to do this is because of the environment variables `SUDO_UID` and `SUDO_GID` that are both set to `0`. This happens because I logged in, elevated to `root`, and then used `sudo -u buildbot -s -H` to perform maintenance tasks under a different user account. ## Expected Behavior Conda should stop messing with filesystem permissions and lying to me. ## Environment Information
`conda info`

issue_title: Problem: Downloading source fails for some packages
issue_body:
I get a Download error on downloading a certain package (patchelf). Its strange, because others work. I'm on latest master 4.3.2+34

  ```
[..] python3.5/site-packages/conda/gateways/download.py", line 99, in download
    downloaded_bytes=streamed_bytes)
conda.CondaError: Downloaded bytes did not match Content-Length
  url: http://nixos.org/releases/patchelf/patchelf-0.9/patchelf-0.9.tar.gz
  target_path: /[..]/conda-bld/src_cache/patchelf-0.9.tar.gz
  Content-Length: 191502
  downloaded bytes: 768000
```

The downloaded file in the src_cache is ok, md5sums match. Running conda build a second time works because it takes it from the src_cache. So I guess its a problem in the downloader. I could reproduce this error with this file only by now. Changing tar.gz to tar.bz2 (which is also offered by this vendor) makes it work. Other packages with tar.bz2 and tar.gz work fine though.

The number looks strange, its too perfect.. maybe an overflow or smthg?

In any case: Maybe its useful to clean up the src_cache after error (guess thats a conda-build issue then), because if the download would be corrupted for real, then we'd end up with a corrupted file that gets picked up next time...

issue_title: dependency issues when using channels indexed by conda index
issue_body:
Hi everyone,

first let me thank you for that awesome piece of software you are writing! 
While testing a custom channel I encountered some issue with indexing that channel. I have tried to install a package named msgpack from the custom channel and I got an output like:

```
(P21603)david@xxx:/tmp/conda_env_creator4$ conda install msgpack
Fetching package metadata: ..
Solving package specifications: .
Error:  Dependency missing in current linux-64 channels: 
  - msgpack -> python 2.7

Did you mean one of these?

    python, ipython

You can search for this package on anaconda.org with

    anaconda search -t conda python 2.7

You may need to install the anaconda-client command line client with

    conda install anaconda-client
```

Well actually it isn't true that python 2.7 is missing as a depedency. The selected env reads as:
```
(P21603)david@xxx:/tmp/conda_env_creator4$ conda list
# packages in environment at /tmp/conda_env_creator4/envs/P21603:
#
openssl                   1.0.2d                        0    defaults
pip                       8.1.1                    py27_1    defaults
python                    2.7.11                        0    defaults
readline                  6.2                           2    defaults
setuptools                20.3                     py27_0    defaults
sqlite                    3.9.2                         0    defaults
tk                        8.5.18                        0    defaults
wheel                     0.29.0                   py27_0    defaults
zlib                      1.2.8                         0    defaults
```

The conda version of the system is:
```
             platform : linux-64
        conda version : 4.0.5
  conda-build version : 1.20.0
       python version : 2.7.11.final.0
     requests version : 2.9.1
```

If I try to install msgpack from defaults channel however, it all works flawless. I did some research and believe to found the root of that problem in the repodata.json files. 

The file from from https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/free/linux-64/repodata.json for the package is:
```
    "msgpack-0.2.3-py27_0.tar.bz2": {
      "build": "py27_0",
      "build_number": 0,
      "date": "2014-08-22",
      "depends": [
        "python 2.7*"
      ],
      "license_family": "Other",
      "md5": "4d66130cc34f3270c0ec46d13373bba7",
      "name": "msgpack",
      "requires": [],
      "size": 102353,
      "version": "0.2.3"
    },
```

The repodata  for the exact same file created with "conda index ..." is in contrast:
```
  "msgpack-0.2.3-py27_0.tar.bz2": {
    "arch": "x86_64", 
    "build": "py27_0", 
    "build_number": 0, 
    "md5": "4d66130cc34f3270c0ec46d13373bba7", 
    "mtime": 1408732571.0, 
    "name": "msgpack", 
    "platform": "linux", 
    "requires": [
      "python 2.7"
    ], 
    "sig": null, 
    "size": 102353, 
    "ucs": 4, 
    "version": "0.2.3"
  }, 
```

One may note that there is a missing * after "python 2.7" in "requires" in the latter json snippet. This missing * seems to do the trick. After adding it manually the installation from the local custom channel worked. 

Finally let me point out that the behaviour of "conda index" is theoretically correct. The index.json file of msgpack-0.2.3-py27_0.tar.bz2 is:
```
{
  "arch": "x86_64", 
  "build": "py27_0", 
  "build_number": 0, 
  "name": "msgpack", 
  "platform": "linux", 
  "requires": [
    "python 2.7"
  ], 
  "ucs": 4, 
  "version": "0.2.3"
}
```
Therefore it appears that the problem is determined by the package itself. But maybe you could add some fault tolerance to the indexer. That would be really great!

Thank you and kind regards!

issue_title: Invalid root_dir for pip installed conda on ubuntu 14.04
issue_body:
These lines https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/4b51cd79bf17f59d1c1db95eeae539b4831baba9/conda/config.py#L115-L116 expect sys.prefix be the same as conda's prefix.  This is not true on ubuntu 14.04.

```
$ which python
/usr/bin/python
$ which conda
/usr/local/bin/conda
```

As a result, the symlink in an environment points to /usr/bin/conda, which does not exist.  The if-condition in https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/4b51cd79bf17f59d1c1db95eeae539b4831baba9/conda/install.py#L387-L388 is True and the os.symlink call will raise an exception due to "OSError: File exists".

It seems like this can be workaround by setting CONDA_ROOT=/usr/local

issue_title: conda env export under python2 is ug
Strange representation of OrderedDict in iPython
issue_body:
I am not even sure if this is an issue, though I found this behaviour misleading and it actually differs from other environments I have used. I am using: **Python 3.6.1 |Anaconda 4.4.0 (x86_64)** and **IPython 5.3.0**. 
```
from collections import Counter, OrderedDict 

class F(Counter, OrderedDict):
   pass
words = ['hello', 'my', 'hello', 'world', 'absd']

F(words) # result F({'absd': 1, 'hello': 2, 'my': 1, 'world': 1})

```
While both repr(F) and str(F) both return correct representation: **"F({'hello': 2, 'my': 1, 'world': 1, 'absd': 1})"**

I think the correct behaviour is that it should use **__repr__**, otherwise it's quite misleading.

issue_title: conda spending much time looking through files in package cache (conda 4.3.13)
issue_body:
Hi - 
this is kind of hard to look for in the issues so I apologize in advance if this has already been brought up.  Recently, any conda operation has been extremely slow. For example, a simple conda search takes 5 minutes:

```
$ time conda search pysam
[...snip...]
real    4m58.644s
user    3m3.074s
sys     0m46.633s
```
This was with 3 channels active. Nearly the same time with just the defaults channel. As a quick and dirty way to try and figure out what is happening I did an strace with time stamps
```
strace -fro conda_search_strace conda search pysam
```
That ran for 272s
```
$ awk '{t+=$2}END{print t}' conda_search_strace
272.496
```

Most of that time (2/3) is spent stat-ing files in the package cache:
```
$ grep 'stat' conda_search_strace | grep '/usr/local/Anaconda/pkgs' | awk '{t+=$2}END{print t}'
178.849
```

Our package cache is large (68GiB - it's a central install used by admins and users to create many environments so it's hard to clean up) and located on a more-or-less busy NFS mounted file system.  Indeed at less busy times this operation runs faster, though still 2 minutes, which is still somewhat painful.

So my questions are (1) did something change in conda that would explain this slowdown? (2) is there anything we can do? Or conversely - are we doing something to cause this? (3) could whatever conda does that requires touching everything in the package cache be optimized? caching? database?

Anyway - please forgive me if i missed something obvious.

```

issue_title: conda --remove corrupts ~/.condarc
issue_body:
`conda --remove` seems to write out YAML files w/o leading whitespaces in lists, a format that conda's config file reader can't read (as mentioned in #632).

Here's how to reproduce:

```
[mjuric@gallifrey ~]$ cat ~/.condarc
channels:
  - http://eupsforge.net/conda/nextgen
  - defaults

[mjuric@gallifrey ~]$ conda config --remove channels http://eupsforge.net/conda/nextgen --force

[mjuric@gallifrey ~]$ cat ~/.condarc
channels:
- defaults

[mjuric@gallifrey ~]$ conda config --add channels http://eupsforge.net/conda/nextgen
Error: Could not parse the yaml file. Use -f to use the
yaml parser (this will remove any structure or comments from the existing
.condarc file). Reason: couldn't parse modified yaml
```

issue_title: Issue installing mayavi in fresh install of Anaconda
issue_body:
I am using Ubuntu 16.04, 64-bit. 

I have just installed Anaconda (Anaconda2-4.4.0). The first thing I tried to do was running the following command:
`$ conda install mayavi`

And I got the following output:
```
Fetching package metadata .........
Solving package specifications: .

UnsatisfiableError: The following specifications were found to be in conflict:
  - mayavi
  - navigator-updater
  - pywavelets
Use "conda info <package>" to see the dependencies for each package.
```

As you can see, the error doesn't really explain anything. I had to do this to actually install mayavi:
```bash
$conda create --name mymayavi python=2
$source activate mymayavi
$conda install mayavi
```

Why can't I install mayavi with a simple `conda install` when I have a completely fresh anaconda installation? 

Does the error mean that I cannot have `navigator-updater` and `pywavelets` installed to install mayavi? If that's the case, or other case, please make this output more readable.

issue_title: ability to get direct access to tag buffer (no decoding to python list)
issue_body:
Hi!  Thanks for pysam, it's handy!

We (PacBio) use tags that encode arrays of per-base features.

In pysam, the `AlignedSegment.opt` method gives access to the tag values, but it immediately converts our arrays to lists.  This is unfortunate, because processing python lists is dead slow; even *converting* them back to numpy arrays is very slow.

Is there a way you could expose a raw buffer interface to tags values with array types? (or maybe tag values in general?), or perhaps `array` types instead of lists?

https://docs.python.org/2.7/c-api/buffer.html
https://docs.python.org/2/library/array.html

issue_title: API suggestions for VariantRecordSample: attributes for sample being no call, heterozygous, etc.
issue_body:
This is similar to what PyVCF already provides and makes looking at samples more convenient. Some examples that come to mind:

- `called` boolean to distinguish between called (0/1, 0/0, ...) and non called (./.) sites;
-  A boolean to determine if the sample is of reference genotype or is variant (PyVCF uses `is_variant` but I'm not too sold on that)
- Likewise a boolean to determine if the locus is heterozygous or not.

My use case: largish VCF files from tumor analyses where due heterogeneity I have a certain proportion of missing calls across samples for a determined locus.

Currently I have to look every sample and check GT or see if `alleles` are just the reference allele, and it's a bit ugly.

issue_title: pysam breaks with Cython 0.25
issue_body:
Cython 0.25 has updated `Cython.Distutils.build_ext`, which has broken pysam with the following error on OSX, Linux and likely all OSes and Python versions:

```
    File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/pysam/__init__.py", line 5, in <module>
    from pysam.libchtslib import *
ImportError: dynamic module does not define init function (initlibchtslib)
```

The problem is that the source is `pysam/chtslib.pyx`, yet the extension is named `pysam.libchtslib`.  For reasons I don't completely understand, the module init function behaves differently even though both versions of Cython create the same module init function `initchtslib` and under Cython 0.25 the above error results.  Or more importantly: I don't understand how this worked at all under previous versions of Cython.

This problem is a _big deal_, since pysam is going to break as soon as Cython is upgraded and it is rebuilt.  I have a PR ready for a fix that has been tested on OS X and will shortly be tested on Linux for Python versions 2.7.12, 3.4.5 and 3.5.2 and Cython versions 0.24.1 and 0.25.  Rather than renaming the chtslib.{pyx,pxd} source files to libchtslib.{pyx,pxd}, I've reworked how internal modules link to the chtslib shared library.  Maybe this is the right thing to do -- it certainly makes some of the internal logic in setup.py less hairy -- but maybe it isn't.  The consequence is that external modules that need to link against pysam will have to change yet again.

issue_title: Delete prompts should accept yes/no
issue_body: I really hate the delete prompts and find the --yes parameter a little awkward; but if we must have it, then the command line prompt should accept yes/no as responses, which is standard *nix behavior.

issue_title: KNACK CognitiveServices module conversion
Throttling problems
issue_body:
We have a build that relies heavily on using azure storage via the CLI.
A major thing that bugged me is that from time to time I get errors due
to throttling.  Recently this turned from an occasional annoyance to a
major issue, since we now have builds that fail because of throttle
related problems.

Solving these problems in a robust way as far as I can currently see is
difficult enough[*] that it's practically impossible.  There should
really be a flag for "just make it work, wait whatever time it takes for
it".

I think that such a mode should be the default, but just a flag would be
a huge help.

[*] The way I could think about resolving it now is to run the command
and capture its stdout and stderr separately; inspect the stderr for
things that *look* like throttle problems (ie, check for a magic word in
the stderr); and if there is such a problem, guess some time to sleep
and re-try (sometimes there's information in the stderr, but parsing
that would be very brittle); if not then spit out the saved stdout.

issue_title: The confirmation parameter should be remove from kwargs instead of passing on to the command.
issue_body:
When `confirmation` is required for a command, a `confirmation` parameter is added to the command to accept `--yes` switch. The parameter value is then stored in the keyworded parameters. As a result of the value, the command function must define a parameter to accept the value even through itself never handles the confirmation process.

Suggestion: remove the parameter from the keyworded parameters before they are sent to the command.

Reference: 
[1] https://github.com/Azure/azure-cli/pull/2509
[2] https://github.com/Azure/azure-cli/blob/1917ba7dc7583bfc0b66e2757cb0e98eb353bdf2/src/azure-cli-core/azure/cli/core/commands/__init__.py#L409
[3] https://github.com/Azure/azure-cli/blob/1917ba7dc7583bfc0b66e2757cb0e98eb353bdf2/src/azure-cli-core/azure/cli/core/commands/__init__.py#L346

issue_title: Restructure azure-cli package (and command module loading)
issue_body:
The azure-cli package should be a thin entry point to the CLI.
This package will depend on 'azure-cli-core' which holds logic for commands, outputs, etc.
This package can then have dependencies on any command packages that it may need.

Command packages should depend on a package called 'azure-cli-core' to allow command packages to register commands, etc.

This will provide a clear interface between command packages and the core of the CLI.

 For reference this is how we install the cli:

sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get install -y libssl-dev libffi-dev python-dev
echo "deb [arch=amd64] https://apt-mo.trafficmanager.net/repos/azure-cli/ wheezy main" | sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/azure-cli.list

sudo apt-get -y update && sudo apt-get install -y azure-cli
```

Like I said, we do this in automation (aka it's a clean VM every time) and we haven't changed anything about how we install the CLI. I tried interactively installing with the `https://aka.ms/InstallAzureCli` command and did _not_ reproduce this error.

NetworkManagementClient not in azure.mgmt.network.network_management_client

I've installed the network package like this: `pip install azure-mgmt-network==0.20.0`, then using `ipython` when I do:

In [1]: from azure.mgmt.network.network_management_client import NetworkManagementClient

So I went on the source code looking for that `network_management_client` and found out:
```
$ git clone https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python

issue_title: Regex's for us/ga/*.csv is allowing bad addresses
issue_body:
The regexes ```^([0-9]+)( .*)``` and ```^(?:[0-9]+ )(.*)``` are applied to the STR field to extract the house number and street fields.  Unfortunately there are some source entries that are not that simple, leading to bad export entries.  Some source examples:

```
-81.61218255758295,31.445076249046316,"0 PENNICK RD BRUNSWICK 31525","glynn","ga"
-81.60793125629436,31.4285784352523,"310 VERNON LEWIS LN Unit: 54 BRUNSWICK 31525","glynn","ga"
```

and their corresponding export entries:

```
-81.5599224,31.3246046,0,Pennick Road Brunswick 31525,,,ga,,
-81.6079313,31.4285784,310,Vernon Lewis Lane Unit: 54 Brunswick 31525,,,ga,,
```

In these cases "Brunswick 31525" are the city/postalcode in GA containing those addresses.

This seems to only affect the following sources:
glynn.csv (~44491 entries)
brantley.csv (39 entries)
camden.csv (62 entries)
mcintosh.csv (7 entries)

issue_title: Metahooks
issue_body:
I'd like to be able to configure pre-commit to run a hook, which itself specifies one or more other hooks to run, along with their configuration.

Consider the case of an organization with multiple repositories which wants to enforce a single `yapf` configuration between them.  Today, each repository has to include something like the following stanza in its configuration file:
```
-   repo: git://github.com/pre-commit/mirrors-yapf
    sha: v0.21.0
    hooks:
    -   id: yapf
        args:
        - -i
        - --style=...
```

Instead, it would be useful to be able to have a git repository with an entry in its `.pre-commit-hooks.yaml` which looks something like this:
```
-   id: my-org-hooks
    name: Hooks for my organization
    metahooks:
    -   repo: git://github.com/pre-commit/mirrors-yapf
        sha: v0.21.0
        hooks:
        -   id: yapf
            args:
            - -i
            - --style=...
```

The individual projects could then just put the following in their `.pre-commit-config.yaml` files:
```
-   repo: git://myrepo.git
    sha: ...
    hooks:
    -   id: my-org-hooks
```

In this case, `my-org-hooks` would run each of the entries in `hooks` as a separate hook with the given arguments, reporting the results independently, perhaps with the following output:
```
Hook 1...............................................(no files to check)Skipped
Hook 2...................................................................Passed
Hooks for my organization:
  yapf...................................................................Passed
```

Any organization-wide changes could then be applied to the centralized location, rather than having to be changed everywhere.

issue_title: Configuration v2
issue_body:
A bit of an RFC
## Issues

There's some "problems" with the current configuration and I'd like to address those in a backwards incompatible way (probably through a migration period where both formats are allowed).

There's two primary issues I'd like to address with the current configuration format:
- The top level is currently a list, making it difficult to add repository-level configuration: #240 #281 (as well as other things I'd like such as repository-level interpreter version)
- The configuration is not scriptably rewriteable: #210

There's also auxiliary issues:
- yaml is not maintained
- yaml has some security issues #396
- `hooks.yaml` isn't well namespaced
## Proposal
### A new configuration language

I shopped around for configuration languages which fit the criteria above, notably:
- Provides a non-C implementation
- Supports comments + maps + lists
- Supports round-trip rewritability
- Has a python implementaiton

And did not find anything.  Given that, I've started speccing and implementing [a configuration language](https://github.com/asottile/dumbconf) with those design goals in mind.  It also aims to be significantly simpler than yaml (the spec may not fit on a postcard like json, but it's also not 70+ pages like yaml).
### New filenames

The names of the files will need to change for two reasons:
- The configuration will no longer be yaml
- hooks.yaml is not a namespaced-enough name

Proposing:
- `.pre-commit-config` - describes the consumer configuration (previously `.pre-commit-config.yaml`)
- `.pre-commit-manifest` - describes the repository configuration (previously `hooks.yaml`)
### New configuration structure

The new configuration structure will be a top-level mapping type to allow repository-wide configuration.  This map will have a `hooks` key which will contain the current configuration.

``` yaml
hooks: [
        ...,
]
```

This will allow for top-level configuration which will apply to all hooks (such as global excludes, interpreter versions, etc.)
## Migration
- The new configuration will be introduced.
- In that release and the next two releases (or the next 3 months, whichever comes sooner), pre-commit will load both configuration formats but issue warnings for the legacy format.
- After the migration period, the old configuration format will be purged.

The "officially" maintained repositories ([pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks](https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks) as well as the [mirrors-*](https://github.com/pre-commit) repositories) will force-push tags for the lastest $n versions and provide both configuration formats for those tags.  Going forward, [pre-commit/pre-commit-mirror-maker](pre-commit/pre-commit-mirror-maker) will only produce the new configuration format.

To assist in migration (though the formats should be relatively easy to manually migrate) a tool will be created `pre-commit-migrate` which will attempt to take the legacy format and produce new configuration files.  Given yaml's lack of rewritability this will likely reformat lists and drop comments, but for the last time!

Thoughts?

issue_title: Too small number of iterations in fit() method
issue_body:
Hi

It seems that `ConvergenceMonitor` stops HMM fitting 1 iteration too early. For a given HMM, its `monitor_` is initialized as:

    class ConvergenceMonitor(object):
        def __init__(self, tol, n_iter, verbose):
            (...)
            self.iter = 1

`self.iter` is incremented only in `report()` function, which is called _between_ E and M steps during fitting. Right after it, convergence is checked:

    def fit(self, X, lengths=None):
            (...)  # E step here
            self.monitor_.report(curr_logprob)  # monitor_.iter is incremented
            if self.monitor_.converged:
                break
            (...)  # M step here

So, during `n'th` iteration, `monitor_.iter` is being set to `n` before performing M step. Therefore, during 1-before-last iteration, convergence check reports that iterations number limit was reached... 

    @property
    def converged(self):
        """``True`` if the EM-algorithm converged and ``False`` otherwise."""
        return (self.iter == self.n_iter or  # THIS CONDITION STOPS FITTING
                (len(self.history) == 2 and
                 self.history[1] - self.history[0] < self.tol))

...and fitting is stopped.

Maybe `monitor_` should be initialized with `self.iter = 0`?

Regards
Michał

issue_title: (X-)Forwarded not compatible with AF_INET6
issue_body:
Hello,

The X-Forwarded and Forwarded middleware is not compatible with sockets created by AF_INET6. As per the [sockets](https://docs.python.org/3/library/socket.html#socket-families) documentation, for AF_INET6 a 4-tuple is used. The current [middleware](https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp-remotes/blob/master/aiohttp_remotes/x_forwarded.py#L94) only handles 2-tuple case when sockets are created by AF_INET.

Since we only require the peer_ip, we would just take the first item of the tuple returned.

issue_title: Trusted certs pulled over insecure channel
issue_body:
Currently certifi is pulling the ca-bundle over a non-TLS protected channel (http), which means that anyone between the host and ci.kennethreitz.org can inject or completely modify the ca certificates bundle to inject their own trusted ca, allowing them to man-in-the-middle tls traffic between python clients and any site they connect to. 

To address this, the ca bundle should be hosted on a site protected by TLS, and https://github.com/certifi/python-certifi/blob/master/tasks.py#L5 should be updated to reflect the new endpoint.

issue_title: [Linux] Cache files in $XDG_CACHE_HOME/tldr instead of ~/.tldr_cache
issue_body:
According to the [XDG Base Directory Specification](https://specifications.freedesktop.org/basedir-spec/basedir-spec-0.8.html), user-specific cached files files should be stored in `$XDG_CACHE_HOME/tldr`, or `$HOME/.cache/tldr` if `$XDG_CACHE_HOME` is not defined.

> There is a single base directory relative to which user-specific non-essential (cached) data should be written. This directory is defined by the environment variable $XDG_CACHE_HOME. 

This avoids cluttering the home directory and simplifies including/excluding related directories from bulk operations such as [backups](https://github.com/puniverse/capsule/issues/104).

### Links
- [XDG Base Directory support - Arch Wiki](https://wiki.archlinux.org/index.php/XDG_Base_Directory_support)
- [XDG_CACHE_HOME equivalent on Mac OS X - Stack Overflow](https://stackoverflow.com/a/5084892)

### Repos
- [jleclanche/python-xdg](https://github.com/jleclanche/python-xdg)
- [wijnen/python-fhs](https://github.com/wijnen/python-fhs)

issue_title: Directory issue in "Validate" if notebook_dir is not home directory
issue_body:
### Operating system
Debian/stretch
### `nbgrader --version`
0.6.0.dev0 (github version with PR #880 merged)
### `jupyterhub --version` (used with JupyterHub)
0.8.0b5 
### Notebook version info
5.1.0
### Expected behavior
Successful or unsuccesful validation of notebook instead of error message
### Actual behavior
Teachers and students have notebooks not in home directory, but in $HOME/notebooks. This is specified in jupyterhub config file as:
`c.Spawner.notebook_dir = '~/notebooks'`  (we use LocalProcessSpawner).
For example, as a teacher (mike) I have this course root directory:
`/home/mike/notebooks/uvp17_mike` and the assignment subdirectory `mojtest` with notebook `Zadanie.ipynb` which I like to validate. Opening this notebook in jupyterhub ad trying "Validate" button results in error message.  This is what I get as part of traceback:
`File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/nbgrader/utils.py", line 234, in chdir
    os.chdir(dirname)
FileNotFoundError: [Errno 2] No such file or directory: 'uvp17_mike/source/mojtest'`

I can confirm that after copying course files to home directory, the validation is successful.
So this issue can be related to #855 which @jhamrick fixed by PR #866 (`Set nbgrader url prefix to be relative to notebook_dir`).  Before formgrader, the `nbgrader` commands for generating, releasing, validating and collecting assignments were  executed in the course root directory, so those problems with paths were hidden. Now it is necessary for all the formgrader-related activities  to be aware of the change from PR #866. I suspect, this is not the curent situation, which can give raise to many path-related issues.   

### Steps to reproduce the behavior
Try `nbgrader quickstart` to generate course in a subdirectory of home directory (e.g. `$HOME/notebooks`) using jupyterhub configured with that subdirectory as `notebook_dir`. After that, try to validate test notebook in `ps1` assignment directory by `Validate` button.

issue_title: Formgrader Jupyterhub Process Giving 404s for CSS/JS
issue_body: Just wanted to put this into the issues in case it comes up for others. There is an issue with the new method of using fromgrader on a JupyterHub instance as a managed jupyter notebook server. This seems to be a JupyterHub issue, not an nbgrader issue, but I wanted to make other users aware. I've described it [here (issue #997)](https://github.com/jupyterhub/jupyterhub/issues/997) along with a workaround. If you start the managed JupyterHub process and are unable to view it, check your web console and see if you get `custom.js` and `custom.css` 404s. If so, see the the referenced issue.

issue_title: Maintainable Themes
issue_body:
I was part of the discussion found here: https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/jupyter/sMXF3kPmvXg
As part of a project I'm working on, Malti(https://hackaday.io/project/5081-malti), I need to write a custom theme for notebook. In the discussion above I learned that people generally go about this the wrong way. I'd like a little feedback from people about the best way to implement maintainable themes.
What I propose is:
1) Setting up a separate theme repo so we don't clutter the notebook repo.
2) Adding a theme menu to the notebook homepage
3) Adding a theme directory to the install files
4) Submitting themes as .less files

To implement the theme menu we could have it display the contents of the theme folder. Once the user selects a theme we run gulp automatically to regenerate the css. If the user has LiveReloading running in their browser it should automatically update.

issue_title: preact, preact-compat, proptypes as bower dependencies vs. npm
issue_body:
Follow up from https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/issues/2056: Users are seeing `error: [Errno 2] No such file or directory: '/PATH/TO/NOTEBOOK/node_modules/proptypes'` in 5.0.0rc1.

The notebook's only npm dependencies after being [unwebpackified](https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/pull/2011) is preact, preact-compat, and proptypes. Is there any reason why we can't get these dependencies from bower? There probably is but I just want to make sure. 

1. Install preact, etc. from npm: https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/blob/271c4b1f279901e06f522a22a2521eb9a9e6c20b/package.json#L22-L23
2. Copy npm packages from `node_modules` to `components` directory: https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/blob/master/setupbase.py#L383
3. Import preact, etc. using script tags: https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/blob/326077fa2d7addd646de2302217f8cff4e9c8d5d/notebook/templates/page.html#L19-L21
4. Use preact, etc. in ShortcutEditor: https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/blob/c305d8fda3b5f8bfb8c1ce520c0a6927f56261a1/notebook/static/notebook/js/shortcuteditor.js#L36-L169

cc @minrk @blink1073

issue_title: matplotlib x axis labels dissapear in scatterplot
issue_body:
Please take look at http://stackoverflow.com/questions/43121584/matplotlib-scatterplot-x-axis-labels or https://github.com/Kornel/scatterplot-matplotlib/blob/master/Scatter%20plot%20x%20axis%20labels.ipynb 

When I create a scatter plot with the colours specified using `c="C"` the x axis labels disappear.

```
import pandas as pd
%matplotlib inline
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
%config InlineBackend.figure_format = 'retina'

test_df = pd.DataFrame({
        "X": [1, 2, 3, 4],
        "Y": [5, 4, 2, 1],
        "C": [1, 2, 3, 4]
    })

# This shows the x axis labels just fine
test_df.plot(kind="scatter", x="X", y="Y");

# This does not
test_df.plot(kind="scatter", x="X", y="Y", c="C");
```

The solution is to provide the axes explicitly:

```
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
test_df.plot(kind="scatter", x="X", y="Y", s=50, c="C", cmap="plasma", ax=ax);
```

This looks like a jupyter issue since calling this from "regular" python with savefig does not yield this problem.

issue_title: ignorefiles directory skipping bug
issue_body:
If a user uploads a list of directories and skips any the next directory is also skipped.
The problem is the code that walks directories:
https://github.com/Duke-GCB/DukeDSClient/blob/9d5473baa5bbd17a5f7c89ce8934a0370eb4c5d7/ddsc/core/localstore.py#L126-L132

`os.walk` requires skipped directories to be removed from`child_dirs` to avoid iterating over them.
This removal needs to be done outside of the loop above. https://docs.python.org/2/library/os.html#os.walk

Since the [share/deliver actions in DukeDSClient](https://github.com/Duke-GCB/DukeDSClient/blob/master/ddsc/ddsclient.py#L260) already have a step to look up the user by email or netid, this would be a good time to register the user account if needed.

I was trying to use autograd for a simple matrix factorization. `A (MXN) -> W (MXk). H (KXN)`. Here is the code that I have written. The cost is the frobenius norm of the difference between A and W.H. When I compute autograd on this cost, autograd returns a matrix corresponding to the shape of W. How can we get the gradient value for both W and H?

```python
import autograd.numpy as np
import pandas as pd

A = np.array([[3, 4, 5, 2],
                   [4, 4, 3, 3],
                   [5, 5, 4, 4]], dtype=np.float32)

shape = A.shape #M, N
rank = 2 #k

def cost(W, H):
    pred = np.dot(W, H)
    return np.sqrt(((pred - V).flatten() ** 2).mean(axis=None))

H =  np.random.randn(rank, shape[1])
W =  np.random.randn(shape[0], rank)

# Testing cost

grad_cost= grad(cost)

grad_cost(W, H)

issue_title: Ignore unused imports in a try block with except ImportError
issue_body:
A common pattern is to try to import a module to see if it is installed, so code can fail early with a nice error message when it isn't. Something like

```py
try:
    import matplotlib
except ImportError:
    raise ImportError("matplotlib is required")
```

The `raise ImportError` could also be some code that uses a fallback. 

Unfortunately pyflakes gives an unused import warning for blocks like this. You can silence them by rewriting like

```py
try:
    import matplotlib
    del matplotlib
except ImportError:
    raise ImportError("matplotlib is required")
```

but I think this pattern is common enough that pyflakes should just ignore unused imports if the import is inside a try block with an "except ImportError". 

Is there any risk of false negative here? As far as I can tell, the only point of "unused import" warnings is to avoid importing a module that you never actually use, which could add import times and unnecessary dependencies. But in this case, the import is very clearly done intentionally.

issue_title: Agent "Zooming" Too Fast In Simulation Despite Correct Step Count
issue_body:
(Copied from the ProjectMesa Google group let me know if more info needed!)

> I have a single-agent simulation and a grid-world in which the agent uses two navigational strategies to move around. One of these strategies is reactive (it sets a goal, then step by step moves towards it) and the other is deliberative (it plans ahead using many epochs of A* algorithm navigation then moves there). My problem is, whilst reactive works fine, A* zooms madly on the simulation. A* is designed to take a step to plan ahead, which provides a vector of x,y coordinates the agent should follow as a path, then take each of these a step at a time. Instead, even though my code is telling me the agent is taking the true number of steps it should do, the agent zips along super fast along its designated path instead of moving at a regular 'step-by'step' pace. Which really throws off my external timer!
> 
> There are of course a lot of other components to this system, but hopefully this is enough information for someone to provide some help. Am I cramming too much into one step, and that's why the simulation is playing catch-up or something? The A* planner does take a second or two in some cases as it sometimes generates thousands of planning steps in one go. Do I just not have a sufficient understanding of how the mesa.time module works?

My current working branch of this project can be found at the master [here](https://github.com/seifely/mesa_boxworld); merged it recently so it would be easier for someone helping to sort through. **The agent should be run on 'navigation_mode=2' for it to use the A* algo, which I have set as the branch default.** If any more description or comments are needed please let me know and I can add some!

issue_title: Add note to docs about pytest-cov and pytest doctest support
issue_body:
A coworker of mine was shocked that the built-in pytest doctest support module didn't integrate with pytest-cov. He wrote a shim to get things working. I was shocked too: I am pretty sure  things already work, if you use the arguments in the right combination.

Seems like the gist is just to make sure you have "--doctest-modules" and "--cov" arguments, in your command (or config file).

like ...

```bash
py.test foo --cov=foo --doctest-modules
```

(*EDIT* -- my previous command example WORKED but it was misleading as to what was going on. command should now be more clear.*)

I think he was forgetting `--cov` argument and confusing himself. A hint in the docs might help folks avoid this blunder going forward.

So maybe it would be good to just include a usage example like the above, in some docs somewhere. When you look for "doctest" in the pytest-cov docs you don't find anything. Maybe this little FAQ or footnote should be stuffed somewhere, so that searching the pytest-cov docs for "doctest" brings up a hint.

Happy to make a PR... just unclear where to stick this.
