issue_title: Enum issue during Python 3 setup
issue_body:
There is a subtle issue in setup.py that is causing problems for me in Python 3 on Travis. 
The problem is with the line: https://github.com/ndexbio/ndex2-client/blob/master/setup.py#L79
```
'enum; python_version == "2.6" or python_version=="2.7"'
```
It turns out that when you build a wheel the environment setting gets stripped off. Later, when installing the wheel with pip, the environment setting is not there, and pip tries to install `enum` even on Python 3, leading to an error.

I am working on a patch for this, I'll submit a PR soon.

**Referenced assemblies which are set to Copy Local are not copied to the output directory**

I've described this in [this StackOverflow question](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/47952361/assembly-referenced-in-csproj-is-not-copied-to-output-directory) too.

I want to work with Azure Storage. I've added Nuget package references, this way:

```
<ItemGroup>
	<PackageReference Include="WindowsAzure.Storage" Version="8.7.0" />
	<PackageReference Include="Microsoft.WindowsAzure.ConfigurationManager" Version="3.2.3" />
</ItemGroup>
```

However, when I build the project (which is a web project by the way), I receive an error that it can't find the required DLL files from these packages. When I look at the output folder which is `bin\Debug\netcoreapp2.0\` in my case, I see that no DLL from those packages exist in that folder.
The point that it can't find those DLL files means that something is not working properly. We expect by including a package reference in CSPROJ, all of the required actions be taken care of automatically.

So, I decided to add extra lines of code, to explicitly copy the required DLL files to the output folder:

```
<ItemGroup>
    <Reference Include="Microsoft.WindowsAzure.Configuration">
        <HintPath>..\Microsoft.WindowsAzure.Configuration.dll</HintPath>
        <Private>true</Private>
    </Reference>
    <Reference Include="Microsoft.WindowsAzure.Storage">
        <HintPath>..\Microsoft.WindowsAzure.Storage.dll</HintPath>
        <Private>true</Private>
    </Reference>
</ItemGroup>
```

But they are not getting copied. What's the problem?

issue_title: HelpfulArgumentParser should know about flags that are relevant to several topics
issue_body: We currently have a bunch of hairy special cases where flags are documented in different places depending on which help topics are being displayed.  These are bad to maintain, so we should either have HelpfulArgumentParser know about and organise them automatically (alternatively, we chould completely rearchitect cli.py to give good concise topic-based help in some better way, such as writing our own argument parser, forking [config]argparse in the tree, or something else..).

issue_title: Don't show users Augeas paths
issue_body:
Users have reported seeing errors like the following:
```
Cannot find a cert or key directive in
/files/etc/apache2/sites-available/ssl.conf/virtualhost.
```
This is an Augeas path and I think it is as likely to confuse users as it is to help them. This and any similar output should be changed to show the actual file path.

issue_title: why func in renewal.conf?
issue_body:
I see this line in `renewal.conf`:

```
func = <function obtain_cert at 0x30c9500>
```

I just want to make sure that's leftover debugging junk and not something used by the client.

I'm working on a [node.js letsencrypt client](https://github.com/Daplie/node-letsencrypt-cli) and I'm trying to make sure that my config file is as exactly compatible with the python as possible.

Obviously that's not portable and there's nothing I can do about it, but as long as the python client doesn't actually use it there's not a problem. Otherwise I'd like to file a bug on it and see if it can be represented in some other way.

issue_title: better way to confirm domain ownership, standalone custom port, letsencrypt IP range redirection?
issue_body:
Currently the only viable options for me are using

1. Webroot mode
Requires me to make superflous webroots on my webserver such as mail.foo.com, imap.foo.com smtp.foo.com and many other things which have nothing to do with my web-server.

2. Standalone mode
[*] Requires me to shut down my webserver for a few seconds, interrupting customers. Not good at all.

Neither of these options are very appealing.
What if we can use a non-standard port for standalone mode, such as port 79 or something?

Alternatively can we get a list of letsencrypt IP addresses, so that I can redirect letsencrypt connections seeking port 80 to port 79?

There's gotta be some clean solution?

[*] This can be very dangerous because currently letsencrypt-auto often wants to go into interactive mode unexpectedly. If this were part of a script then the webserver would stay down permanently while letsencrypt waits for user response.

issue_title: Simple-verify for all plugins. Log, do not fail.
issue_body: Let's use `logger.warning` instead of stopping the client when `self_verify` has failed. Also, let's do it for all plugins, not just manual authenticator. It might be opt-out, but I don't see a clear reason why it should be.

issue_title: Default RSA key bitlength should be 3072
issue_body:
[According to NSA](https://www.nsa.gov/ia/programs/suiteb_cryptography/index.shtml) and [ANSSI](http://www.ssi.gouv.fr/uploads/2015/01/RGS_v-2-0_B1.pdf), RSA with 3072 bit-modulus is the minimum to protect up to TOP SECRET.

We should not be in the red line of cryptography. This security by default will bring the needed protection for all the Internet users that pass by HTTP webservers powered with Let's Encrypt.

issue_title: Problem with sending emails with emojis
issue_body:
I got an issue with emails that contain emojis. When I receive email with emojis
all emojies are displayed like a bunch of '??'. I tried to send the same data via Postman
and email I received wasn't broken. So it seems someting wrong happens on the way 
between this python client and API server.
By digging into source code of `Transmissions` part and by sniffing traffic
I found that python's json module encodes (dumps) 'not simple' non-ascii characters 
(such emojies) to '\uXXXX' notation as a surrogate pair (as expected):

```
>>> emoji = u"😄"
>>> emoji
u'\U0001f604'
>>> print json.dumps(emoji)
"\ud83d\ude04"
```

By sending this data I got this emoji looks like:

```
\xed\xa0\xbd\xed\xb8\x84
```

And it's of course wrong. I sniffed traffic between Postman and API server and I found
that Postman sends json in utf-8 (without escaping to \uXXXX):

```
\xf0\x9f\x98\x84
```

So I repeat this in Python and it worked:

```
>>> json.dumps(emoji, ensure_ascii=False).encode('utf-8')
'"\xf0\x9f\x98\x84"'
```

I don't know on what side problem is, but this is my workaround to handle this problem.
And this Issue is just a point on a problem and its possible solution.

PS. There is no problem with sending emails consist of simple chars like '\u2605'.

issue_title: api_version is set to 1 for v0.10
issue_body:
I am trying to understand why our Java Kafka Stream application was complaining on `Extracted timestamp value is negative` and I came to capture/debug the packets of `confluent-kafka-python`. I realised that although I was setting the `'api.version.request': 'true'` in the producer, the `Produce Request` and `Produce Response` packets had `api_version` set to 1. Moreover, wiresharks kafka dissector was complaining with malformed packet in v0.10 (when `'api.version.request': 'true'`) only on `Produce Request`.

I have experimented with the following version:

| librdkafka      | confluent-kafka-python           | `'api.version.request'`  |
| :------------: | :----------------------------: | :----------------------: |
| 0.9.2-RC1      | 0.9.2                                          | _not set_    |
| 0.9.3              | 0.9.2                                          | _not set_  |
| 0.9.3              | 0.9.2                                          |   `'true'`    |

In all cases the api_version is equal to 1 and the packet is malformed only in v0.10 case.
1) I understand that the kafka dissector may not be update for the latest Kafka version but the packet structure for the Produce Request is the same in all versions
2) Produce Response are not the same in all versions because the api version 2 (v0.10) has `timestamp => INT64`, but the dissector is not complaining on their packet format.
3) The api_version is 1 in all tries, I was expecting to see 2 in v0.10, correct?

I got my understanding on packet format from the [protocol page](http://kafka.apache.org/protocol.html#protocol_messages).

I am attaching the three packet captures: [packet_captures.zip](https://github.com/confluentinc/confluent-kafka-python/files/771793/packet_captures.zip)

So my question are:
1) Should I have api_version set to 2 on v0.10 messages when I set `'api.version.request': 'true'`?
2) In this the reason that I don't get timestamped messages on Kafka Stream application, after the broker?
3) Do I need to set other flags for the timestamp-ing of messages for version v0.10?

Thanks a lot for your time! :)

issue_title: bash() in replwrap.py expects bash prompt to end with $ or #?
issue_body:
I was playing around with a Jupyter Notebook with the metakernel_bash kernel, and I noticed that the kernel took an unusually long time to load, and the notebook wouldn't work as expected (i.e., not like a bash prompt). In the log, I was getting an error message having to do with shell_magic.py. 

After some digging around, it seems that bash() in replwrap.py expects my bash prompt to end with $ or #. I had a custom prompt that didn't end in either of those characters. Changing my custom prompt to end with $ solved the problem.

I'm not sure what's the best way to solve this - just thought you should know.

issue_title: Simplify setting values
issue_body:
It would be nice, to simplify `set_value` by allowing to set values using proxy objects: 

```python
# alternative syntax for madx.set_value(NAME, VAL)
madx.globals[NAME] = VAL

# Set properties directly on elements
elem = madx.sequences[SEQU].elements[ELEM]
elem['knl'] = [VAL0, VAL1]

# and even: (using another proxy object for the list)
elem['knl'][0] = VAL0
```

Setting element attributes is strictly more powerful than the existing `Madx.set_value` method - at least I didn't find any syntax to set the contents of arrays like KNL so far. This requires to implement an `ElementProxy` object, that sets the property directly on the actual C element object rather than using `Madx.input`, which will also require some clever IPC marshaling. Furthermore, I am not even sure yet, whether changes on the element fields are correctly propagated.

```

#define TURI_LOGGER_FAIL_METHOD(str) throw(str)

issue_title: ENH: calcparams_pvsyst should output d2mutau and vbi
issue_body:
**Problem**
* users may incorrectly use coefficients from `calcparams_desoto` with `d2mutau` in `singlediode`
* is there a way to restrict or limit use of `d2mutau` only for coefficients derived from `calcparams_pvsyst`?
* users may not correctly account for the correct number of cells in series and the the number of junctions per cell when determining builtin voltage parameter
* the `cells_in_series` argument in `bishop88` is redundant and inconsistent with the other argument with series cells `nNsVth`.

**Solution**
As @cwhanse suggested, replace `voltage_builtin` with `NsVbi` which is an output from `calcparams_pvsyst`, and `d2mutau` should also be passed through `calcparams_pvsyst` in order to discourage users from using it with CEC or DeSoto models.

>We currently pass the product `nNsVth` into `singlediode` and related functions, rather than passing `n`, `Ns`, and `Vth` separately. With the recombination current, the only place `Ns` is needed separately is with the per-cell `Vbi` to get a module `Vbi`. I think a more consistent method would be to pass `NsVbi` as the argument, rather than `Vbi`. That keeps all the single diode equation parameters as 'per-module' quantities, rather than a mix of per-cell and per-module.

**Alternatives**
Please suggest alternatives. One idea that was proposed originally was I think to lock in specific usage with the model, ie: have a separate `singlediode_desoto` and `singlediode_pvsyst` similar to the `calcparams_*` methods. Not sure how far down this needs to go, would it also apply to `bishop88`?

**Additional context**
this is an follow on to #504

issue_title: Ingredient configurations that depend on other configuration entries
issue_body:
I'd like to have ConfigScopes inside an Ingredient that depend on other config entries. I define my Ingredients in separate files so I can import them from different Experiment scripts. I define a ConfigScope inside the Ingredient that depends on the config entry `a`, e.g.

```
ing = Ingredient('ing')

@ing.config
def config(a):
    b = a/4
```
If I call the experiment from command line like `python experiment.py with ing.a=8` everything works fine, but if I want to define `a` inside the experiment script with `ing.add_config(a=8)` it sais that  `a` is missing (because the ConfigScope of the ingredient is executed before the ConfigScope added in the experiment script).

I came up with prepending the config to the config scopes like this:
```
ing.add_config(a=8)
ing.configurations.insert(0, ing.configurations.pop())
```

Is there a better way to do this?

issue_title: Resolve security group names in the SecurityGroup definition
issue_body:
As a follow up for https://github.com/zalando-stups/senza/issues/357 

Unfortunately the changes don't help to define a new SecurityGroup that refers to an existing one. E.g. I need to create a security group (as a part of my stack). As a very essential part of the group I need to enable ssh from the Odd host. After the Odd host has become a part of its own stack, its security group name appears as a dynamic name that looks like `Odd-OddSecurityGroup-14OUA2EZ2C3RO`. To refer to it w/o editing the Senza YAML every time, I need to do this:
```
      Type: AWS::EC2::SecurityGroup
      Properties:
         GroupDescription: ElasticSearch master or data node
         Tags:
            - Key: "Name"
              Value: "{{Arguments.AppId}}"
         SecurityGroupIngress:
         -  IpProtocol: tcp
            FromPort: 22
            ToPort: 22
            SourceSecurityGroupName: "{{Arguments.OddSG}}"
     ...
```
Where `OddSG` argument is defined as a result for ad-hoc query:
```
ODD_GROUP_ID=$(aws cloudformation describe-stack-resources --region ${REGION} --stack-name Odd |
               jq -r '.[][] | select(.LogicalResourceId == "OddSecurityGroup") | .PhysicalResourceId')
```
It looks really ugly :(

Is it possible to use some custom attribute name (instead of `SourceSecurityGroupName`) to make Senza to lookup the name (by the tag 'Name', of course)?

issue_title: EasyId3: Add album artist-mapping
issue_body:
Originally reported by: **timpulver (Bitbucket: [timpulver](http://bitbucket.org/timpulver), GitHub: [timpulver](http://github.com/timpulver))**

----------------------------------------

The album artist tag `TPE2` is pretty standard, so I think it’s a good idea to add it to the default mapping.  

`EasyID3.RegisterTextKey("albumartist", "TPE2")`  

`TPE2` is not by definition meant to be used as album artist tag, but nearly every program around uses `TPE2` as album artist.

References of TPE2 as album artist tag:

- [MP3Tag](http://help.mp3tag.de/main_tags.html)
- [Stackoverflow](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/5922622/whats-this-album-artist-tag-itunes-uses-any-way-to-set-it-using-java)
- [Hydrogenaudio](https://www.hydrogenaud.io/forums/index.php?showtopic=85165)

Btw. I’m very happy EasyId3 has a mapping for catalognumber and publisher / label :)

----------------------------------------

issue_title: Quantifier instantiation not happening
issue_body:
Mate Schwerhoff previously reported a bug on codeplex (see [here](http://z3.codeplex.com/workitem/190)), migrating this to the new issue tracker. In his own words:

Given the program below, Z3 4.3.2 and 4.4.0 55ca6ce44b2f (both Win x64) immediately report unknown, despite the fact that the given terms and patterns should allow it to instantiate each forall once, thereby showing that the goal is unsat.
(set-option :auto_config false) ; IMPORTANT
(set-option :smt.mbqi false)    ; IMPORTANT
(set-option :smt.qi.profile true)

(declare-fun fun (Int) Int)
(declare-fun funLim (Int) Int)

(declare-const x Int)

(assert (forall ((x Int)) (!
  (= (fun x) (+ 1 (funLim x)))
  :pattern ((fun x)))))

(assert (forall ((x Int)) (!
  (= (fun x) (funLim x))
  :pattern ((fun x)))))
  
(assert (not ; goal
  (= (fun x) (+ 1 (fun x))))) ; Change second fun to funLim and Z3 reports unsat
  
(check-sat) ; unknown
(get-info :all-statistics) ; no forall instantiated (if funLim is used, both are instantiated once)
Am I missing something here that would explain why Z3 doesn't perform the instantiation, or is this a bug in the triggering code?

issue_title: Speedup constraint solving process
issue_body:
Hello,


I am working on a symbolic executor to analyze some program, and I am writing to ask for help on speeding up the constraint solving of `Z3 Java`. 

What I am basically trying to do is to iterate the whole program, symbolize some variables, and collect certain path conditions along the code. When encountering certain criteria, I will pause my symbolic executor and put all the collected path conditions (they are `BoolExpr`) into the solver and let it check whether there exist a satisfiable solution regarding the symbolized variables. (of course it is indeed more complex but I try to simplify the presentation..)

 I give one path condition example below, in which `symbol1` is the symbolized variable. As you can see, the formula itself is not that large, but according to my observation, when I have have around 10~20 such kind of formulas, the solver takes several seconds to check the satisfiability.

`(134821342,(let ((a!1 (ite (= ((_ extract 2 2) symbol1) #b1)
                #x00000002
                (ite (= ((_ extract 1 1) symbol1) #b1) #x00000001 #x00000000))))
(let ((a!2 (ite (= ((_ extract 4 4) symbol1) #b1)
                #x00000004
                (ite (= ((_ extract 3 3) symbol1) #b1) #x00000003 a!1))))
(let ((a!3 (ite (= ((_ extract 6 6) symbol1) #b1)
                #x00000006
                (ite (= ((_ extract 5 5) symbol1) #b1) #x00000005 a!2))))
(let ((a!4 (ite (= ((_ extract 8 8) symbol1) #b1)
                #x00000008
                (ite (= ((_ extract 7 7) symbol1) #b1) #x00000007 a!3))))
(let ((a!5 (ite (= ((_ extract 10 10) symbol1) #b1)
                #x0000000a
                (ite (= ((_ extract 9 9) symbol1) #b1) #x00000009 a!4))))

                #x0000000e
                (ite (= ((_ extract 13 13) symbol1) #b1) #x0000000d a!6))))

                #x00000010
                (ite (= ((_ extract 15 15) symbol1) #b1) #x0000000f a!7))))
(let ((a!9 (ite (= ((_ extract 18 18) symbol1) #b1)
                #x00000012
                (ite (= ((_ extract 17 17) symbol1) #b1) #x00000011 a!8))))
(let ((a!10 (ite (= ((_ extract 20 20) symbol1) #b1)
                 #x00000014
                 (ite (= ((_ extract 19 19) symbol1) #b1) #x00000013 a!9))))
(let ((a!11 (ite (= ((_ extract 22 22) symbol1) #b1)
                 #x00000016
                 (ite (= ((_ extract 21 21) symbol1) #b1) #x00000015 a!10))))
(let ((a!12 (ite (= ((_ extract 24 24) symbol1) #b1)
                 #x00000018
                 (ite (= ((_ extract 23 23) symbol1) #b1) #x00000017 a!11))))
(let ((a!13 (ite (= ((_ extract 26 26) symbol1) #b1)
                 #x0000001a
                 (ite (= ((_ extract 25 25) symbol1) #b1) #x00000019 a!12))))
(let ((a!14 (ite (= ((_ extract 28 28) symbol1) #b1)
                 #x0000001c
                 (ite (= ((_ extract 27 27) symbol1) #b1) #x0000001b a!13))))
(let ((a!15 (ite (= ((_ extract 30 30) symbol1) #b1)
                 #x0000001e
                 (ite (= ((_ extract 29 29) symbol1) #b1) #x0000001d a!14))))
(let ((a!16 (bvxor #x0000001f
                   (ite (= ((_ extract 31 31) symbol1) #b1) #x0000001f a!15))))
(let ((a!17 (bvshl symbol1
                   (bvand (concat #x000000 ((_ extract 7 0) a!16)) #x0000001f))))
(let ((a!18 (ite (= (bvand (bvadd a!17 a!17) (bvadd a!17 a!17)) #x00000000)
                 #b1
                 #b0)))
  (= a!18 #b0))))))))))))))))))))
`


Of course seconds are acceptable, however, eventually the path conditions could grow quite large, and there exists hundreds or even thousands of them.. I do observed that the solver becomes much slower regarding those more challenging situations... 

So here is my question, given my situation (solving hundreds median/large size constrains with one or several symbols),  how should I start to speedup the solving process? I can come up with several options:

1. Cache the already solved constrains to save the re-solving time ==> I implemented such strategy in my tool

2. Substitute some large composite formulas with multiple symbols into a fresh new symbol ==> this can speedup the process in sacrifice of false positive.

3. some other things..?

I do not know much about theory proving and constraint solving, and I am sorry if these seems too naive.. I am writing to ask, could you please sled light on some general approaches to speedup the solving process in my case? Any suggestion is appreciated. Thank you!

issue_title: Validate that indicator patterns use valid observable types and property names
issue_body:
Issues like oasis-open/cti-stix2-json-schemas#55 are caused because we don't validate the contents of Indicator pattern expressions, just the syntax. We should be able to combine the [stix2patterns.inspector module](https://github.com/oasis-open/cti-pattern-validator/blob/master/stix2patterns/inspector.py) with the data in `enums.py` to check whether object types and properties (including via referenced objects) are valid.

For custom types and properties, we can follow the same rules as the rest of the validator:
- allow them regardless if they follow the correct naming conventions, and 
- raise an error if they don't, which can be ignored/silenced.

To scope this, I'll assume the following are out of scope for now:
- Checking if the values are the right type (since this isn't yet tracked in enums.py)
- Allowing users to specify names of properties of custom objects in or nested property names in custom properties. So basically, as soon as a (valid) custom object name or property name is encountered, checking that component of the pattern stops.

issue_title: Optimization of walk.files()
issue_body:
Use case:

- S3FS file system
- Looping over files in S3 bucket
- Many (thousands) file are stored on the same directory level

Current implementation of Walker.walk.files() doesn't use benefits of iterator when many files are stored in the same directory. It actually loads all files into memory and returns it to application at once. With S3 file system it may take minutes before the application actually receives first item to process.

I propose the following changes:

1. Add the following method to `class Walker`

        def _walk_files(self, fs, path, namespaces=None):
            """Walk files using iterator.
    
               This method allows to scan efficiently directories with large amount of files
               as it doesn't collect files in a temporary list.
    
               Using a *breadth first* search 
            """
            path = abspath(normpath(path))
            queue = deque([path])
            push = queue.appendleft
            pop = queue.pop
    
            while queue:
                dir_path = pop()
                for info in self._scan(fs, dir_path, namespaces=namespaces):
                    if info.is_dir:
                        if self.check_open_dir(fs, info):
                            push(join(dir_path, info.name))
                    else:
                        if self.check_file(fs, info):
                            yield (dir_path, info)



2. Modify `WalkerBase.files()` method as:

        def files(self, fs, path='/'):
            """Walk a filesystem, yielding absolute paths to files.
      
            Arguments:
                fs (FS): A filesystem instance.
                path (str, optional): A path to a directory on the filesystem.
      
            Yields:
                str: absolute path to files on the filesystem found
                recursively within the given directory.
      
            """
            iter_walk = iter(self._walk_files(fs, path=path))
            for dirname, info in iter_walk:
                yield join(dirname, info.name)




This will allow looping of files in S3 bucket efficiently.

Note, above suggestion uses a hard-coded *breadth first* search algorithm. It may be modified to support *depth first* search as well.

issue_title: It's all about the DOM
issue_body:
We have to make a difficult choice in how we handle cell output.

The current notebook lets cell output do anything with the page, including modifying the notebook itself and running code on kernels. This is both powerful and a large risk which is overcome by restricting cell output to `<iframe>`s or even [Electron's `web-view`](https://github.com/atom/electron/blob/master/docs/api/web-view-tag.md). That ends up meaning you get consistent state on the notebook and all the UI while restricting what can be mutated by cells. On the other hand, this also means that cells can't communicate with each other. It's a tradeoff!

issue_title: Exception on session-close
issue_body:
It looks like that ncclient throws an exception on session-close, but it's impossible to tell what went wrong from the exception message which is basically an empty string. Here's the code:

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python

from ncclient import manager

def connect(host, port, user, password, source):
    print('establishing connection...')
    conn=manager.connect(host=host,
                         port=port,
                         username=user,
                         password=password,
                         timeout=10,
                         hostkey_verify=False,
                         device_params={'name':'default'})
    try:
        print("connection has been established")
    finally:
        print("closing connection...")
        try:
            conn.close_session()
        except Exception as sessionException:
            print("An exception occurred while closing the session :" + str(sessionException))
        print("connection has been closed")


if __name__ == '__main__':
    connect('localhost', 1830, 'user', 'pass', 'candidate')
```

When I run this script I get the following output:

> 
> establishing connection...
> connection has been established
> closing connection...
> An exception occurred while closing the session :
> connection has been closed
> Exception in thread session (most likely raised during interpreter shutdown)

I think it's worth mentioning what server and nccclient send to each other on session-close event. Nccclient sends the following XML on session-close event:

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<nc:rpc xmlns:nc="urn:ietf:params:xml:ns:netconf:base:1.0"
        message-id="urn:uuid:ded644f4-4787-456c-84b2-b24e6c2eace9">
  <nc:close-session/>
</nc:rpc>
```

The server responds with the following XML document:

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8" standalone="yes"?>
<ns2:rpcReply xmlns:ns2="urn:ietf:params:xml:ns:netconf:base:1.0" message-id="urn:uuid:ded644f4-4787-456c-84b2-b24e6c2eace9">
    <ns2:ok xmlns:xsi="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema-instance" xsi:type="rpcOk"/>
</ns2:rpcReply>
```

After that ncclient throws an exception and abruptly closes the connection to the server.

issue_title: RQAlpha 当前 Universe 在 tick 级别策略中的逻辑 bug。
issue_body:
RQAlpha 当前 tick 逻辑如下：

> 策略 Universe 中的合约会触发 tick 事件，tick 事件一方面触发合约最新价格变化，另一方面触发 handle_tick。

在此逻辑下，若策略的持仓中有未订阅的合约，则该合约对应持仓的 holding_pnl，total_value 等不会动态更新，导致策略持仓、账户等计算错误。

可行的解决办法：
1.  将 Universe 拆分，在 rqalpha 层和策略层维护两个 universe，rqalpha 层的 universe 是用户 universe 以及持仓合约的并集。该方案不会改变现有 universe 的概念和逻辑，但会影响性能，且比较丑陋。
2. 更改 universe 逻辑，发生交易时自动帮策略订阅合约，且策略不可取消订阅当前持仓的合约。该方案可能会导致老策略的运行结果与之前相比发生变化，但逻辑上比较合理。

issue_title: Ganga not closing automatically after running in batch form
issue_body:
I have a simple ganga script which downloads Dirac files from a given job (ganga-getfiles.py - code below). I have been calling this in a bash script which loops over several jobs and performs a few simple operations, like this (code simplified for clarity):

for j in `seq 200 300` ; do
    ganga ../common/ganga-getfiles.py $j
    hadd myoutfile_${j}.root <gangadir>/${j}/*/output/file.root
    rm <gangadir>/${j}/*/output/file.root
done 

(where the python script ganga-getfiles.py is just a few ganga commands to grab root files from the grid for this job $j).

I have observed that if the script ganga-getfiles.py fails to download any Dirac files (e.g. if there are no matching files, or the job doesn't exist) then ganga will not automatically exit - it prompts me to answer:

"Job status update or output download still in progress (shutdown not completed after 5 seconds). 6 background thread(s) still running: ['SessionLockRefresher', 'JobRegistryFlusher', 'TaskRegistryFlusher', 'JobRegistryFlusher', 'JobRegistry_Monitor', None]. Do you want to force the exit (y/[n])?" 

which clearly causes problems when trying to perform automatic tasks. Is this expected behaviour? If not, what might be causing it?

Many thanks,
Mark

==== ganga-getfiles.py ====

`from Ganga.GPI import *
import sys
import inspect
import os


if len(sys.argv) not in (2,3):
    sys.exit("Script requires the id of the job to use as inputdata and optionally name of a file containing LFNs to process.")

jobnum = int(sys.argv[1])


for sj in jobs(jobnum).subjobs.select(status='completed'):
    		for f in sj.outputfiles.get(DiracFile):
        		if f.namePattern.endswith('BGun.root'):
            			if f.lfn:    #ensure file has lfn
                			f.localDir = sj.outputdir
                			f.get()`

issue_title: Status for a group
issue_body:
Assign a status to an elements group (eg. hosts group). This status is the worst state of all the elements in the group.

Indeed, an hosts group is a group of hosts and an host do not have any state. We have a livestate item that is linked to the host and it is this livestate item that holds the current host state. The idea behind this is to create a *fake* group of livestate items linked to the corresponding hosts/services group.

issue_title: rs_deproject_pixel_to_point()  elucidation
issue_body:
Hello GoodEvening to everybody,
I'm looking at the source code of  rs_deproject_pixel_to_point() function,

https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/blob/master/include/librealsense/rsutil.h
line 43 in particular;

`float f = 1 + intrin->coeffs[0]*r2 + intrin->coeffs[1]*r2*r2 + intrin->coeffs[4]*r2*r2*r2;`

why coeffs[4]? It should be coeffs[2], in that formula it must consider the radial distortion coefficents instead. coeffs[4] is instead a tangential distortion coefficent.

Am I wrong? Can anyone help me to understand this function (and in particular the used formula) ?

thanks for your help.

issue_title: Support for PEP 526 - Use type hinting to express field types
issue_body:
I really like Odin and I have used it before.
There's one thing that is missing from the library that I would love to have.
I'd like to be able to declare my models and mappings using type hints.
[pydantic](https://pydantic-docs.helpmanual.io/) provides a way to do so but it does not provide mappings.
Resources can be defined like so:
```python
from typing import Dict
import odin

class Author(odin.Resource):
    name: str

class Book(odin.Resource):
    title: str
    author: Dict[str, Author]
    genre: odin.String(max_length=255) # Only types with constraints have to be defined using custom types which are subclassed from the actual types
    num_pages: odin.Integer(min_value=1)
```

Mapping can be defined like so:
```python
import odin

class CalendarEventToEventFrom(odin.Mapping):
  source: CalendarEvent
  target: CalendarEventFrom

  event_date: odin.From(CalendarEventFrom.start_date)

  # Mapping to multiple fields
  @odin.map_field(to_field=('event_hour', 'event_minute'))
  def start_date(self, v):
        # Return a tuple that is mapped to fields defined as to_fields
        return v.hour, v.minute
```

We can come up with more syntactic sugar to replace what we currently have.
I'm aware that Odin still aims to support Python 2.7 so we'd have to find a way for this support to co-exist with the current implementation.

issue_title: Add support for testing a Pulp agent
issue_body:
An "agent" can be installed on a system. This agent allows Pulp to send commands to that system. See: http://pulp.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dev-guide/architecture.html We should be able to test interactions between a consumer (a system with an agent) and a Pulp server.

Let's build out support for this scenario. One option is to change how we use the `settings.json` file. (The file is badly named. It should be named `server_configs.json`, or something like that.) Currently, that file may have a single "default" section. We can change the semantics assigned to that file and expect at least a "server" section, corresponding to a Pulp server, and optionally allow for the presence of a "consumer" section, corresponding to a system with the Pulp agent installed. Modules could then choose whether or not to run based on the presence or absence of this config file section. Here's a verbose example of what could be in a test module:

```python
def setUpModule():  # pylint:disable=invalid-name
    """Skip tests if there is no system running the Pulp agent."""
    set_up_module()
    if 'agent' not in config.ServerConfig().sections():
        raise unittest2.SkipTest(
            'This module requires a system running the Pulp agent.'
        )
```

Also see: https://github.com/PulpQE/pulp-smash/compare/master...Ichimonji10:test-agent

issue_title: Patching mbedtls - vrs IDEs
issue_body:
There's an IDE based issue with patching mbedtls that IDEs cannot easily handle.

In the ./configure world - (typically linux, but also cygwin, or msys) one often installs the package called: "build-essentials" and/or other sw-development packages or bundles. In the end, the "patch" command is present. Life is good, and simple.... and joyful.

IDEs do allow for a a pre/post build command. But the trouble is the way the are implemented. What they  typically have is one single dialog box you an enter your commands, typically 1 command. Some but not all support 1 command per line.  This feature is often implemented via the standard linux C library function  system()  [see: man 3 system]

If the command is a script... and often it is a script. Creating a platform independent script is not possible, IDEs do not have a script language, nor do they have a "dos-command" vrs "linux" vrs "cygwin" type dialog box.  Macro names in these commands vary greatly, ie: IAR uses $VAR$ with two dollar signs, Others use the posix shell variables, and windows batch files use %NAME%

We cannot demand/require that a WINDOWS user has Python installed and in their $PATH$ - as often developers have multiple versions of python,  (I have 5 versions, it is painfully complex and too far off topic to describe the reasons here)

Putting tools like CYGWIN or MSYS in their default path will often cause countless other problems, as those tools are very pseudo-environment specific.

While we can launch and IDE from a batch file - IDEs are normally not launched from a batch file (that script would for example modify the $PATH variable, or set other variables). The customer (or in this case: our victim) normally double-clicks an ICON and expects it to work.  

What about "git apply patch"?  While some IDEs include builtin support for GIT ... in some cases it a exotic version of git... (eclipse: jgit) which is not available via the command line.

It gets wierder, and complicated supporting windows users:  They have  Eclipse GIT (with its own settings), there is "git-bash" - with its msys based settings,  and Tortoise GIT, downright painful.

**I see two options here:**

**Option 1:**
The best option for an IDE is to have the mbedtls package "prepatched" and ready to go.

i know this is counter to the idea of a "virgin and untouched" third-party sub directory, my counter would be:  Rename the directory:

for example:  ${openthread}/thirdparty/mbedtls 
Becomes: ${openthread}/thirdparty/mbedtls.patched

And provide a top level script in thirdparty that can re-apply the patch to a new version of the 3rd party package.

**Option 2:**
Is Do this some other way,  and I'm stuck here. 
I'm open to ideas and need some suggestions.

issue_title: Limitar los títulos de los datasets a 100 caracteres
issue_body:
**Contexto**

La API de CKAN da error al intentar federar un dataset cuyo título tiene más de 100 caracteres, si bien esto sí se permite desde la experiencia visual (una de las rarezas de CKAN).

No nos parece mal, de todas maneras, ajustarnos a este límite para tener títulos de datasets más concisos.

**Propuesta**

Esta validación es _muy sencilla de agregar_ se hace desde el _schema_ en JSON que lee la librería `jsonschema`.

En la línea de configuración de la propiedad `title` del esquema `dataset` (https://github.com/datosgobar/pydatajson/blob/master/pydatajson/schemas/dataset.json#L35) probablemente haya que agregar un par clave/valor nuevo que limita la cantidad de caracteres del campo a 100.

Aprovecho este issue sencillo para que @lrromero se familiarice con la librería de validación: https://python-jsonschema.readthedocs.io/en/latest/

issue_title: Sequence length inference is wrong for the embedding layer
issue_body:
The following two lines are the code to infer the length of the incoming sequences. 
https://github.com/tflearn/tflearn/blob/b1a34ae7293ed3e388b079e857ba362028da6e9b/tflearn/layers/embedding_ops.py#L69-L70
I think it is not correct. If I guess it correctly (it is not documented), zero is reserved and can not be used as an index of the sequence tokens. Then, the inferred length is 
```python
inference.seq_length = retrieve_seq_length_op(tf.expand_dims(incoming, axis=2))
``` 

Here is an example to show the current code not work when retrieve the `seq_length`. 
```python 
import tensorflow as tf 
import tflearn
import numpy as np 
import sys 

embedding_dim = 30
num_vocab = 10
batch_size = 5
max_seq_len = 7

seq = tf.placeholder(tf.int32, shape=[None, max_seq_len])
embedding = tflearn.layers.embedding(seq, input_dim=num_vocab, output_dim=embedding_dim)

feed_dict = {seq: np.random.randint(3, size=(batch_size, max_seq_len))}

tf_config = tf.ConfigProto(device_count = {'GPU': 0})
with tf.Session(config=tf_config) as sess:
    sess.run(tf.global_variables_initializer())
    sess.run(tf.local_variables_initializer())
    embeds = sess.run(embedding, feed_dict=feed_dict)
    try: 
        sess.run(embedding.seq_length, feed_dict=feed_dict)
    except Exception as e:
        print('='*80 + '\nAn error occurred: \n\n{}\n'.format(e) + '='*80)

assert embeds.shape[0] == batch_size
assert embeds.shape[1] == max_seq_len
assert embeds.shape[2] == embedding_dim
```
P.S., I don't think it is a good idea to infer sequence lengths for the embedding layer, nor for the recurrent layers. the infer ops waste a lot of computing as for most case, the users have already know the lengths of the data they feed to the network when they prepare the data. To infer the lengths, it perform two reduction operations on axis 2 and 1, respectively, which is ugly. It's much clear to ask the user to provide these lengths as a parameter of the layers.

issue_title: TFLearn Memory Leak
issue_body:
I'm running the following Flask application to train a DNN similar to AlexNet on some images. After each run I delete everything Python has available to me in memory. However, each run adds about 1GB of memory to the running Python process and produces an OOM exception sooner or later. Below is the entire application, each call to /build runs the model 30 times are random data. This is a proof of concept for a model which has far too much data to read into memory so I'm sampling the data over iterations like this. I know I can use the Image Preloader, but because I have so much data I'm actually loading pickle files in each iteration and not image files at the moment. Either way, some memory continues to be allocated over each iteration of this application which I cannot deallocate. 

`import tflearn
from flask import Flask, jsonify
from tflearn.layers.core import input_data, dropout, fully_connected
from tflearn.layers.conv import conv_2d, max_pool_2d
from tflearn.layers.normalization import local_response_normalization
from tflearn.layers.estimator import regression

import numpy as np

app = Flask(__name__)

keep_prob = .8
num_labels = 3
batch_size = 64


def reformat(dataset, labels, num_labels):
    if dataset is not None:
        dataset = dataset.reshape((-1, 227, 227, num_labels)).astype(np.float32)
    if labels is not None:
        labels = (np.arange(3) == labels[:, None]).astype(np.float32)
    return dataset, labels

class AlexNet():

    def __init__(self):

        @app.route('/build')
        def build():

            # Building 'AlexNet'
            network = input_data(shape=[None, 227, 227, 3])
            network = conv_2d(network, 96, 11, strides=4, activation='relu')
            network = max_pool_2d(network, 3, strides=2)
            network = local_response_normalization(network)
            network = conv_2d(network, 256, 5, activation='relu')
            network = max_pool_2d(network, 3, strides=2)
            network = local_response_normalization(network)
            network = conv_2d(network, 384, 3, activation='relu')
            network = conv_2d(network, 384, 3, activation='relu')
            network = conv_2d(network, 256, 3, activation='relu')
            network = max_pool_2d(network, 3, strides=2)
            network = local_response_normalization(network)
            network = fully_connected(network, 4096, activation='tanh')
            network = dropout(network, keep_prob)
            network = fully_connected(network, 4096, activation='tanh')
            network = dropout(network, keep_prob)
            network = fully_connected(network, num_labels, activation='softmax')
            network = regression(network, optimizer="adam",
                                 loss='categorical_crossentropy',
                                 learning_rate=0.001, batch_size=batch_size)

            model = tflearn.DNN(network, tensorboard_dir="./tflearn_logs/",
                                checkpoint_path=None, tensorboard_verbose=0)


            for i in range(30):
                data = np.random.randn(10, 227, 227, 3)
                labels = np.random.choice([0, 2], size=(10,), p=[1. / 3, 2. / 3])

                data, labels = reformat(data, labels, 3)

                model.fit(data, labels, n_epoch=1, shuffle=True,
                          validation_set=None,
                          show_metric=True, batch_size=batch_size, snapshot_step=None,
                          snapshot_epoch=True, run_id=None)

            del data
            del labels
            del model
            del network
            return jsonify(status=200)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    AlexNet()
    app.run(host='0.0.0.0', port=5000, threaded=True)`

issue_title: Doc unclear on actual usage scenarios that will work
issue_body:
I've been trying to use Dask-kubernetes to have a local Jupyter notebook on my laptop and run the Dask workers run on a Google Cloud Kubernetes cluster. I've been struggling a bit with the doc to figure out if this is supported, and how to do this, although it may not help that I'm learning Kubernetes at the same time. Then I found this https://github.com/dask/dask-kubernetes/issues/58#issuecomment-377811223 , which made everything a lot clearer: "It [Dask-kubernetes] is designed to be run from a pod on a Kubernetes cluster that has permissions to launch other pods."

So this issue is about clarifying the doc, to help people like me in the future. I'm willing to do the pull request, but we should probably figure out if my understand is accurate first. On top of something like the above sentence (on the doc, and `KubeCluster` docstring), here are the things that would have been useful for me to have the doc clarify:

* kubectl doesn't need to be installed (because the Kubernetes Python API is used directly).. pretty sure this is true also...?
* If .kube/config is configured correctly to be able to launch pods, it will be used, and workers will be launched on the default context. (Is that correct? If yes, it would allow the use case I'm looking for. If not, is there a way to influence the Kubernetes cluster used by Dask-kubernetes?)

If the second bullet is supported, it would also be nice to be able to specify a Kubernetes context other than the default, but that should probably be a separate issue...

Thanks!

issue_title: Importing example project fails on fresh Windows 7 installation
issue_body:
The `cmake_example` project fails to be imported on a fresh Windows 7 installation, throwing the immensely informative exception `ImportError: DLL load failed: The specified module could not be found.`

Upon further inspection, using the [depends](http://dependencywalker.com/) tool for Window DLLs, it seems that this is because `MSVCP140.DLL` cannot be found. As far as I know, this is MSVC's C++ standard library. Is there a way to get around this, or is the only way to solve it to tell users to install the [C++ redistributable libraries](https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/download/details.aspx?id=48145)?

Next to that, while investigating and reading up on this issue, I found that to build a binary Python extension, developers are urged to make sure the standard library DLLs that are linked against to be the same as the ones the official Python was built against (e.g., https://matthew-brett.github.io/pydagogue/python_msvc.html, or http://stevedower.id.au/blog/building-for-python-3-5/). I however found that the pybind11 examples were referencing the VS 2015 DLL, `VCRUNTIME140.DLL` (as they were built on AppVeyor with VS 2015). Next to that, the issue with using the offical compiler Microsoft released to ensure compatibility with Python 2.7 (https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/download/details.aspx?id=44266) is so old that it doesn't support C++11. Any thoughts/reassurances on this? Will the Python extensions that I build this way work, even when I would start using more advanced features of the C/C++ standard libraries that have changed between different DLL versions?


Additional links:
- Test repository: https://github.com/YannickJadoul/Speranza/
- Accompanying AppVeyor builds: https://ci.appveyor.com/project/YannickJadoul/speranza
- Cfr. https://github.com/joerick/cibuildwheel/issues/15
- And thanks/credits/subscription-to-this-issue to @RobinJadoul for looking and thinking along

issue_title: HTTP2 support in Nginx plugin
issue_body: Split from #3640.

issue_title: Certbot should depend on josepy
issue_body: As part of the transition of the JOSE library out of acme into josepy, certbot switched to directly importing from josepy -- however, no dependency on it was added in setup.py.  Even though it should always be co-installed by virtue of the transient dependency from acme, if certbot is directly using it, it should be required for install.

issue_title: Prevent regressions of problems like #4044
issue_body:
The cause of #4044 is that although `setUpClass` wasn't added until Python 2.7, [nose manually runs this method for us](https://github.com/nose-devs/nose/blob/master/nose/suite.py#L308). We should make sure our tests catch problems like this in the future.

Options:

1. Stop using `nose` and rely either on standard Python scripts for running tests or use another program to run our tests.
2. Use another test (perhaps like my hacky script [here](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/pull/4043#issuecomment-272350209)) and make sure it runs regularly on a Python 2.6 system.
3. ???

issue_title: Get our OS packagers on a mailing list
issue_body:
We should reach out to our OS package maintainers and try to get them on a mailing list so we can easily send messages with them. Currently, only our Debian, Fedora/EPEL, and Ubuntu PPA package maintainers are on our `certbot-dev` mailing list. We can either ask others to join `certbot-dev`, make a `certbot-packaging` list, or a `certbot-announce` list.

cc @ohemorange

On FreeBSD, I configured my OpenSSL to not contain SSLv3 anymore, as it's (IMO) deprecated.

But certbot somehow requires SSLv3 baked into OpenSSL to work, else:

    Traceback (most recent call last):

issue_title: Turn on OCSP stapling in the Apache installer
issue_body: The Apache installer should be able to turn on OCSP stapling when installing certs in the fully automatic mode.

issue_title: Remove DNS and DNSResponse from acme.challenges
issue_body: This is cruft from a [very old version of the ACME spec](https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-ietf-acme-acme-00#section-7.4) and should probably be removed.

issue_title: RC override reset doco bug
issue_body:
Hi team

Firstly thanks for this incredible tool. Hoping to contribute to this project.

Was about to file a bug about the rc override not working - doco at http://tridge.github.io/MAVProxy/system.html says to unset an RC override, use

```
> rc all -1
```

or 

```
> rc 3 -1
```

This doesn't work - set an override, then unset, but it will be the same.

```
STABILIZE> rc 3 1000
2013: RC_CHANNELS_RAW {... chan3_raw : 1000, chan4_raw : 1515, ...
STABILIZE> rc 3 -1
2592: RC_CHANNELS_RAW {... chan3_raw : 1000, chan4_raw : 1515, ...
```

However I just realised that 

```
rc 3 0 
```

does work.

```
3229: RC_CHANNELS_RAW {..., chan2_raw : 1508, chan3_raw : 968, chan4_raw : 1515...
```

It looks like this is expected behaviour
https://github.com/mavlink/pymavlink/blob/master/mavlinkv10.py#L1690

```
class MAVLink_rc_channels_override_message(MAVLink_message):
    '''
    The RAW values of the RC channels sent to the MAV to override
    info received from the RC radio. A value of -1 means no change
    to that channel. A value of 0 means control of that channel
    should be released back to the RC radio.
```

issue_title: Can't create ranged rows with obvious syntax "lhs <= expr <= rhs"
issue_body:
As stated in the [corresponding test](https://github.com/SCIP-Interfaces/PySCIPOpt/blob/master/tests/test_linexpr.py#L193), for Python syntax reasons, we would need some parenthesis to express ranged constraints (with both lhs and rhs).

This is already broken in the examples, as discovered in a [SCIP issue](https://git.zib.de/integer/scip/issues/1023).

Also, this fact should at least be highlighted in the documentation.

Maybe, we can also catch the exception that is thrown and work around the issue with some Python magic?

issue_title: Better define semantics and improve NettingChannelLibrary
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

At the moment reading the NettingChannelLibrary code can be confusing. Our smart contracts and especially the [NettingChannel](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/dbe4e4bd9e919d1bfffc12e4d0480aac9a987467/raiden/smart_contracts/NettingChannelLibrary.sol) lie at the heart of our system so they should be optimal and very easy to understand.

## Solution

Go through the code of the contract and make sure that all attributes and struct members are commented with a succinct explanation of what each one represents.

Additionally better define the structs that compose the channel. For example the [Participant](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/dbe4e4bd9e919d1bfffc12e4d0480aac9a987467/raiden/smart_contracts/NettingChannelLibrary.sol#L13) struct contains a lot of data that would better belong to a struct for holding `last_message_data`.

Moreover there are struct members like [`netted`](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/dbe4e4bd9e919d1bfffc12e4d0480aac9a987467/raiden/smart_contracts/NettingChannelLibrary.sol#L361) that we have as part of the participant but are only used at the settlement stage and as such don't need to exist during the entire life of a channel as they take up storage.

If there are other things that we can do to improve on readability, semantics and storage requirements of the contract please add them to this issue.

## Tasklist

- [ ] Add comments to all attributes in the NettingChannel
- [ ] Clearly separate the message data from the participant data. Use multiple structs.
- [ ] Get rid of unneeded storage slots in an attempt to minimize storage needed for opening a channel.

issue_title: Make matrix http requests fail-proof
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

Right now, sometimes, some Matrix http requests can fail due to unknown reasons. These exceptions bubble up and crash the node.

Our bets includes issues with kept-alive connections with the server (an optimization to avoid performing the TCP+TLS handshake on every request) in the connection pool being dropped, maybe related to our usage of monkey-patched gevent + requests, temporary network drops in the client, high load on the server (although the servers were stress-tested and actual load doesn't even get close to the tests, which performed well), rate-limit kicking in during burst periods (e.g. on replay queued messages in startup), and so on.

In any case, these issues should be temporary, and as far as the previous requests (e.g. authorization) performed well, the new ones should eventually go through.
Plain count-based retries are already implemented (https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden-libs/pull/111, https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden-libs/pull/112, #2008) and are currently on 5, but they may kick in too fast (e.g. retrying 5 times in less than 1s), and also under the lock, which may cause it to fetch the previous tcp socket and may fail for again for the same reason, so these solutions are not effective: #2029 https://travis-ci.org/raiden-network/raiden/jobs/410094476

# Proposal

These connection exceptions are always raised under https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden-libs/blob/0f3344ee1e68495235eda33925fa02893c1c2c26/raiden_libs/network/matrix/client.py#L46-L48 , in the form of `MatrixRequestError` (5xx on server-side, e.g. `Bad gateway`) and `MatrixHttpLibError` (general http errors, such as connection timing out or refused) exceptions.

The immediate proposed solution is to handle the errors above, and move from a count-based retry to a time-based one (e.g. retrying for 60s until succeeding, and only if not possible, raise the last raised exception). To avoid overloading the server, a wait between retries (e.g. 1s or exponential backoff) should be implemented, being sure to release the lock meanwhile, to let other requests go through. We need to be keep in mind expectedly long requests, like the `/sync` calls, which long-poll the server for ~30s.

Another possible fix, which may stack with above proposal, `MatrixTransport` can handle all these transport and client exceptions, hold the queue and fire up health check loop with the matrix server until it comes back online or we can communicate with it again, resuming the queues then. It'll need to retrieve all events that may have happened while it was offline. Investigation on how the node and protocol reacts to not being able to communicate for different time lengths is required.

issue_title: End sessions on the Matrix server gracefully
issue_body: `MatrixTransport.stop_and_wait` sometimes sends the logout request to the Matrix server before all interactions with it are done. Keep track of all greenlets related to `MatrixTransport`, also those not directly spawned by itself, and join them before logging out.

issue_title: Support for task engine in interactive mode.
issue_body:
We have merged single-process, multi-process and dryrun workflow executor but it appears infeasible to use batch mode executor for interactive use. The biggest problem is that interactive executor uses a single process that shares all variables so that variables would be persistent across multiple cells. This is fundamentally different from the batch mode.

We should however enhance the interactive executor for it to support task engines so that we can submit jobs from the jupyter notebook interface. This should however be implemented after the tasks can be really separated from the sos process so that sos can exit without killing the running jobs (remaining of #448).

issue_title: Output %preview result in notebook.
issue_body: Compared to print or return value for the last statement, magic preview has the advantage of previewing multiple variables or files, and it sometimes make sense to include the output of `%preview` in the main notebook, even when the side panel is open. I suppose an option such as `%preview --in-notebook` or `%preview --embeded` or `%preview --nb` should work.

issue_title: pulse file created in the future.
issue_body:
Got the following warning when executing a task on a remote host.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/9889312/39611311-66d8386a-4f1c-11e8-8609-a84f57258295.png)

The warning is caused by [this line of code](https://github.com/vatlab/SoS/blob/master/src/sos/tasks.py#L731)

```
        start_stamp = os.stat(pulse_file).st_mtime
        elapsed = time.time() - start_stamp
        if elapsed < 0:
            env.logger.warning(
                f'{pulse_file} is created in the future. Your system time might be problematic')
```
According to [this post](https://unix.stackexchange.com/questions/111877/timestamp-of-newly-created-file-is-in-the-future), the files could be generated on the remote host that has different time than the local host. Not sure how this happens but 

1. Should we compare time between two servers and sync their times?
2. Can sos somehow addresses this warning?

issue_title: Improper step name causes DAG to hang with `sos_step` target
issue_body:
This is a generated fake benchmark example, a cleaner version of #968 : 

[dag_hang.tar.gz](https://github.com/vatlab/SoS/files/1995111/dag_hang.tar.gz)

There are 2 files in it: `hang.sos` and `not_hang.sos`. To reproduce the hang:

```
sos run hang.sos
```

and SoS gets caught in infinite loop. But 

```
sos run not_hang.sos
```

works. The only difference between the two is step name: `test_01` in the hang one, and `test01` in the no hang one. 

As pointed out in #968, there is also a problem when changing the target to using file target. But maybe fixing this problem will lead to fix of that one, so let's focus on this one for now and I'll try reproducing the file target version bug after this one is addressed to.

issue_body:
I have a model like the following:
class MyModel(TimeStampedModel):
    entity_type = models.ForeignKey(ContentType)
    entity_id = models.PositiveIntegerField(db_index=True)
    entity = GenericForeignKey('entity_type', 'entity_id')
    other_field = .....

Now I create entry in the table like following:
my_object = SomeModel.objects.create()
MyModel.objects.create(entity=my_object)

And I query MyModel like:

issue_title: RTO1024/RTO1000 scope driver issues
issue_body:
I have found several problems with the RTO scope driver. 

1. The when using the HD mode on an RTO1024 the bandwidht get's set to 1MHz and cannot be changed from there. 
This would not be problematic if this mode was not needed but it is also used to set the dataformat which uses the same parameter. 

Observe the following difference and not the (spurious) staircase due to digitization in the left figure. 

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/6142932/32517253-1e69d472-c406-11e7-80bd-bc421a5c9c39.png)

Both are the same trace measured. The one on the right is measured by a little hack in the driver. 
The second trace contains a scaling error on the y-axis but I'm sure that can be fixed quite easilty. 
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/6142932/32517319-4e4e5352-c406-11e7-8372-369cce667340.png)

I added a screenshot from the scope itself as reference. 
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/6142932/32517336-623ff820-c406-11e7-802a-d3c9c33c31d3.png)


2. Recent QCoDeS updates make this driver give a warning upon instantiation. IMO this should be fixed.

issue_title: hdf5 formatter cannot write a dataset twice
issue_body:
When calling the `write_copy` method of the dataset on an existing location an error is generated.

A minimal example:

```
import numpy as np
import qcodes
from qcodes.tests.data_mocks import DataSet2D
from qcodes.data.hdf5_format import HDF5Format

qcodes.DataSet.default_formatter = HDF5Format()
    
dataset = DataSet2D()
dataset.write()
newlocation = dataset.location
dataset.write_copy(location=newlocation)
```
And another variation
```
from qcodes.tests.data_mocks import DataSet2D
from qcodes.data.hdf5_format import HDF5Format,HDF5FormatMetadata
qcodes.DataSet.default_formatter = HDF5Format()
    
dataset = DataSet2D()
dataset.write()
dataset.formatter.write(dataset, force_write=True)
```
@core @AdriaanRol

issue_title: implementing a ramp
issue_body:
It is possible to implement a ramp using qc.Loop sweeping a parameter and adding a wait time but I think there is some merit to discussing whether this is the way it should be done

`qc.Loop(gate.voltage.sweep(0, -10, 1)).each(qc.Wait(1)).run()`

- It creates a data set which we probably don't want
- imo its not a very natural syntax
- mostly we want to ramp for every set of a parameter and to be able to choose max ramp speed at the start of the experiment and then never worry about it again which seems like something that could go in an instrument driver and then be used in place of the plain 'set' (so no Loop at all)...

What are people's thoughts on this? It seems like something everyone would use, how should it be implemented and what would the ideal syntax be?

issue_title: Add a way to pass context data to overridden template
issue_body: Hello, Currently there is no way to send context data to a overridden template. For instance if you wanted to add a icon to a button. It would be nice to do something like this in your helper: ```python Button( 'Save', css_class='btn-primary', template='crispy-forms/icon-button.html', template_context={'icon': 'flopy-disk'} ) ``` where icon-button.html looks like: ```html ``` I don't think adding support for this would be too difficult, adding it to BaseInput could look like this (the only lines I've added have "template_context" on them): ```python class BaseInput(TemplateNameMixin): """ A base class to reduce the amount of code in the Input classes. """ template = "%s/layout/baseinput.html" def __init__(self, name, value, **kwargs): self.name = name self.value = value self.id = kwargs.pop('css_id', '') self.attrs = {} if 'css_class' in kwargs: self.field_classes += ' %s' % kwargs.pop('css_class') if 'template_context' in kwargs: self. template_context = kwargs.pop('template_context') self.template = kwargs.pop('template', self.template) self.flat_attrs = flatatt(kwargs) def render(self, form, form_style, context, template_pack=TEMPLATE_PACK, **kwargs): """ Renders an `` if container is used as a Layout object. Input button value can be a variable in context. """ self.value = Template(text_type(self.value)).render(context) template = self.get_template_name(template_pack) context.update({'input': self}) if hasattr(self, 'template_context'): context.update(self.template_context) return render_to_string(template, context. ``` If you think this is a good idea I can add it to the other layout objects and put in a pull request.

**Don't know if this breaks other plugins as I only use this one.**



```
def parse_xml(data, name="XML", ignore_ns=False, exception=PluginError, schema=None):
    """Wrapper around ElementTree.fromstring with some extras

    Provides these extra features:
     - Handles incorrectly encoded XML
     - Allows stripping namespace information
     - Wraps errors in custom exception with a snippet of the data in the message
    """
    if is_py2 and isinstance(data, unicode):
        data = data.encode("utf8")

    if ignore_ns:
        data = re.sub(" xmlns=\"(.+?)\"", "", data)

    data = re.sub("&", "&", data.decode('utf8'))

    try:
        tree = ET.fromstring(data)
    except Exception as err:
        snippet = repr(data)
        if len(snippet) > 35:
            snippet = snippet[:35] + " ..."

        raise exception("Unable to parse {0}: {1} ({2})".format(name, err, snippet))

    if schema:
        tree = schema.validate(tree, name=name, exception=exception)

    return tree

```

issue_title: Tickets - Run Query in Use Saved Query not working in Django 2
issue_body:
I'm using the master branch project in Pycharm Ide; Django 2.0; Python 3.4; PostgreSQL (but it's the same thing using SQLite). I can save the filter but when try to use it is not applied. No message appears next to the 'apply filter' button. I tried on herokuapp demo installation and the same filter is applied normally ... the success message appears at the side of the 'apply filter' button. I have tried logged as superuser and as staff: same problem. Can anyone try to reproduce the situation on own master project installation? Thanks.
** UPDATE: 
This is an encoding problem that reside in the bytes literal prefix ('b) before the query field in helpdesk_savedsearch table. Django 2 saves the filter with that prefix, Django 1.11 instead no. When the filter is read under Python3 with Django2:
`staff.py -> line 834: query_params = json.loads(b64decode(str(saved_query.query)).decode())`
the returned params are empty. Without the bytes literal prefix all its ok. 
Be careful: the run_report function in staff.py contains the same code at line n. 1141.
I have apply a provisory workaround at lines 834 and 1141:
`query_params = json.loads(b64decode(str(saved_query.query).lstrip("b\\'")).decode())`
I'm sorry but I still do not know helpdesk code well enough to find a consistent solution valid for all the various combinations versions of both Python and Django.

issue_title: [V3] Singleton Design Discussion
issue_body:
Red as we know it is an application with various singleton components at its core (literally, in the `core` package). What I'm referring to as singletons are components for which there exist only one instance of per application.

In the core package currently, this boils down to:
 - the bank
 - modlog
 - the Sentry manager
 - the help formatter
 - the cog manager
 - the data manager

There is also the RPC server manager, which is currently being refactored over at #1780, and of course the bot itself, however we're not really talking about the bot class itself here, we're talking about other singletons.

### Current design of these components
As of right now, the design of these singletons is inconsistent. Some use a singleton class, which is instantiated on bot initialisation and attached to the bot as an attribute. This includes sentry, the help formatter, the cog manager, and RPC.

The rest simply use their module as a container for data and as an interface to functions, which can be imported at the top of a module. Python allows us to do this as an alternative to the more "Java-esque" approach of singleton classes. But is it good practice? Well, that's what this issue is about.

#### The singleton class approach
Personally, I see this approach as simpler, easier to maintain, and more extendable. On the other hand, it can be seen as polluting the bot object's attributes. It also means we need access to the bot in order to have access to these components, but thankfully the bot object is very easy to get a hold of.

For the help formatter, this approach is actually forced upon us by discord.py and is necessary to allow anyone to customise the help formatter, hence why I mentioned this approach being more extendable.

#### The singleton module approach
This approach is fairly easy to document and use, as users only need to do an import and they can access all of these singleton components. However, this does lead to more imports than may be necessary, and sometimes I fear one day we're gonna fall upon some horrible circular import problems  within the core package.

### What's better?
Personally I prefer the former approach, but this issue is for others to chip in and add to what I have stated above so we can have some sort of standard moving forward.

#### So when we decide what's better, we should rewrite everything to conform to that?
In short, no, not simply for the sake of conforming to a new standard. There may be a reason down the line for us to switch existing singletons over, but right now it's a risk which is certainly not worth taking - all of the singletons currently function just fine, rewriting them will likely just cause more issues, and we also just don't need the extra work. However, hopefully the outcome of this discussion will serve as a good reference for any future developments.

issue_title: ImportError with html5lib on v0.3.1
issue_body:
Looks like `html5lib` has been introduced as a depency at: https://github.com/python-mechanize/mechanize/commit/75f3690d12b793e05fc118b9944c6753a0dc8a7a

This is easy to reproduce:

```
>>> import mechanize
>>> br = mechanize.Browser()
>>> br.open('https://example.com')
>>> br.select_form('whatever')
```
An easy workaround is just to install the missing library with: `pip install html5lib`. However, I would expect that such internal dependency would be already installed along mechanize.

What about adding the following to `setup()` at `setup.py`?

```
install_requires=['html5lib']
```
Or with a fix version:

```
install_requires=['html5lib==0.999999999']
```

issue_title: In some obscure cases, StopIteration exceptions can be turned into RuntimeErrors when passing through nurseries
issue_body:
There are some very obscure edge cases where using async generators does the wrong thing. For example, this code

```python
import trio

async def fail():
    raise ValueError

async def main():
    async with trio.open_nursery() as nursery:
        nursery.spawn(fail)
        raise StopIteration

trio.run(main)
```

Ought to raise a `MultiError([StopIteration(), ValueError()])`, but instead it raises a `MultiError([RuntimeError(), ValueError()])`. This is because you can't throw a `StopIteration` into an `async_generator`-based async generator (for details [see here](https://github.com/python-trio/trio/pull/228#pullrequestreview-44714634)) – it gets wrapped into a `RuntimeError`. If we let that `RuntimeError` propagate, then `acontextmanager` could turn it back, but here it gets wrapped into a `MultiError` instead so the magic doesn't work.

I guess we could make `acontextmanager` smart enough to look inside `MultiError`s? Or stop using `acontextmanager` and write this context manager directly? Or just not worry about it until we can drop 3.5 support and switch to using native async generators, which don't have this problem? It's *very* unusual to have free-floating `StopIteration` exceptions wandering around like this, and that's the only case that's affected. But I figured we should record the problem in any case.

issue_title: DynamoDBStream.describeStream - TypeError: 'NoneType' object is not iterable
DeleteTopic not implemented in SNS
issue_body:
The SNS DeleteTopic API is not implemented in the [local SNS listener](https://github.com/localstack/localstack/blob/master/localstack/services/sns/sns_listener.py). Expected behavior is that making a DeleteTopic API call will delete the topic and all its subscriptions. Due to the way the SNS provider is implemented this is not the case, and subscriptions will continue to accumulate in the `SNS_SUBSCRIPTIONS` list between DeleteTopic and CreateTopic calls. This causes the SNS provider to send multiple copies of the message to any subscribed queues or HTTP endpoints.

Expected behavior would be for the SNS provider to catch the DeleteTopic API call, and respond by clearing out the relevant entries in `SNS_SUBSCRIPTIONS` per the specifications for DeleteTopic in the [AWS docs](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sns/latest/api/API_DeleteTopic.html) :

```
Deletes a topic and all its subscriptions. 
```

If I have a chance I'll try to submit a PR that fixes this.

issue_title: PRelu version issue
issue_body:
In #907, we decided to align to Numpy broadcasting.  This is a break change to many operators, like PRelu, but we didn't upgrade these OP's version. 

For operator versioning, onnx's policy is:
“If the semantics of an operator is changed in a backwards-incompatible way, you MUST create a new operator”

But #907 forgot to upgrade PRelu OP's version.

issue_title: generalize ${X-yD} parsing to work with a sarra interval.
issue_body:
There is some hard coded parsing that sees a substitution such as:

${YYYYMMDD-1D}  and manually substitutes subtracting 1 day (24*3600) from the current time to calculate the value.  Ideally the code would parse the value after the minus sign as a sarra interval,
so you could enter 1h, 3w, 1m or 4d and it would all be correctly dealt with.

This would enhance consistency with the rest of the configuration language.

issue_title: switch from nmslib to hnswlib api
issue_body:
Accoording to #311 , i want to switch nmslib api to hnswlib api.
My nmslib api code is as below:
```
        nmsl = nmslib.init(method='hnsw', space='l2')

```

I find the parameters of hnswlib are complated for me to understand, such as M, or ef_construction.

How should i set the hnswlib paras?
